featurename: Northern Atlantic wet heaths with Erica tetralix
pressures_info:
The lists of pressures and threats are based on expert judgements and on published sources. The lists were discussed and revised together with a reference group of professionals working with research, administration, and active conservation management in semi-natural grasslands. Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), and more knowledge and data. 

However, the problem with alien invasive species (especially Campylopus introflexus) has increased over the last few years and is likely to escalate in the future (Strand et al. 2018). IO2 is therefore assessed as a medium impact pressure and threat.

A20. Eutrophication effects due to high loads of agricultural nutrients to coastal waters causes less light penetration and risk of overgrowth of filamentous algae, which effects the vegetation. Due to measures for decreasing the nutrient load from agriculture, some water quality improvements has been observed but the conditions are still not sufficient (VISS). According to guidelines this pressure (A20) should be used also when the source (natural or artificial) is unknown. The assessment high is set due to the pressure acting over large areas. The threat is set to medium as the improvement of the measures is expected to be slow.
E02. The Swedish coastal waters are among the busiest ones in the world.  The habitat in the region is partly highly exploited and affected by boat transport. The assessment is set to medium due to the the high rate of transports in parts of the habitat.
F07. Disturbance and noise from jets, boating, kayaking, water skiing etc.  The assessment for the pressure and threat is set as medium as the effects are direct on a medium scale.
F08.  Physical disturbance; exploitation (bridges, marinas, cabling etc.) dredging of fairways and dumping of dredged spoils are directly affecting vegetation and benthic organisms but also indirectly by shading and by degrading water quality (resuspension of sediments). The result of the pressure analysis shows significant impact (12%) on the habitat therefore is the assessment set to medium and also the threat due to continued pressure.   
F20: Activities resulting in discharge of toxic substances such as leakage of toxic hull antifouling (TBT) despite long term ban. There are also signs of general deterioration of fish health (e.g.  Zoarces viviparus)  the reason is unknown, but exposure to unknown toxic substances are indicated.  Point sources from sewage plants and also diffuse leakage of sewer from individual households and summer cottages due to substandard sewage treatment is problematic in certain  coastal areas causing nutrient  (nitrogen) load causing decreased light penetration affecting macrophytic production.  The pressure and threat is set as medium due to the ecological effects of the pressure's as well as threat’s direct impact.  
  G01.  In Swedish coastal areas has the pressure from recreational fishing significant impact on fish populations (SLU aqua). But in offshore areas the pressure from commercial fishing is also of importance. The harvesting of fish resulting in a shortage of large carnivorous fish. This leads to top down effects strengthening the impacts of eutrophication leading to decreased habitat quality for example increased production of filamentous algae competing with macrophytes. Also threatening certain targeted species and populations for instance cod. The pressure is therefore set as high and the threat as high, as there are need for further measures (Bergström et al 2016, Helcom, Olsson et al).
 L04. Internal load of nutrients (phosphorus) in offshore Baltic limits the effects of decreased terrestrial load of nutrients and acts on a large scale. However, the effects on the specific habitat is uncertain why the assessment is set as medium. 
I04. The predation from seals and cormorants can have a significant local effect on fish populations the overall contribution to fish population conditions is still unclear. Predation on perch (Perca fluviatilis) and pike (Esox lucius) juveniles from sticklebacks (Gasterosteus aculeatus) has lately been problematic in the outer parts of archipelagos but are slowly spreading to the inner parts. This might be one cause of the slow recovery of the carnivorous fish populations along the Baltic Sea. The assessment is set as low for pressure but medium as threat due to its increasing trend.   
Xu. Unknown cause for general fish health deterioration along the coastal Baltic Sea. The habitat is considered as an important fish nursery and therefore is the assessment for the threat set as high. There is a need for increased knowledge as well as measures in place.

The pressures and threats that act on the sturgeon species have been complied based on the results from the long-running research (over 8 years) on the behavior and migration routes of sturgeon species made by the INCDPM-Bucharest (Deak Gy. et al., 2014, 2017, 2018; Danalache T., et al. 2017). Poaching proved to be  most aggressive problem that affects the conservation state of the sturgeon species. The Acipenser ruthenus individuals that were tagged for monitoring and stopped being detected by the monitoring stations lead us to the conclusion that they might have been illegally fished (Deak Gy. et al., 2014, 2017, 2018; Danalache T., et al., 2017, 2018; Raischi M., et al., 2017, INCDPM – all the research studies from 2011-2019).

pressures_info:
The species habitat is old-growth spruce forests close to the tree border. It is therefore threatened by forestry management, in particular forestry clearance and even the removal of selected trees. Reduction of the habitat of the species is mainly due to forestry and maybe also by grazing effects of reindeers.
Climate change is in the future expected to affect the species habitat in a negative direction. This is an assumption based on forecast for climate change and effects on alpine forest close to tree-line.

pressures_info:
The habitat of the species has gradually declined during the 20th century. The species is found in dry habitats, on the mainland in Sweden mostly on coastal granite bedrock, on the Gotland on limestone. The habitat has deteriorated due to changes in management of dry and nutrient poor grassland, i.e. abandonment followed by overgrowth and finally turned into forest.
On the island Gotland the impact of threats and pressures is still low. The threats and pressures with highest impact act on mainland. 
Future pressures are similar to the present threats, with one exception. Future pressures also include increased exploitation for recreation in coastal areas.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info: The habitat of C. cerdo has decreased enormously in Sweden during the last two hundred years. Millions of old oaks have been cut mainly during the 19th century. Former grazed woodlands are no longer grazed and have turned into forests and others have been cut and replaced with plantations. This is probably the main explanation for the dramatic decrease of the species in Sweden. At present there is only one remaining Swedish population of the species; in the nature reserve of Halltorp on the island of Öland. At this site the main pressure is low population that can result in inbreeding. Also lack of suitable habitat within normal flying range (agricultural as well forest). Other local pressures/threats are predation by crow birds and in the future collection.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info:
The lists of pressures and threats are based on expert judgements and on published sources. Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), and more knowledge and data. 
However, the problem with alien invasive species has increased over the last few years and is likely to escalate in the future (Strand et al. 2018). I02 is therefore assessed as a medium impact threat.

pressures_info: La gineta no presenta problemas de conservación evidentes. La especie no cuenta con amenazas importantes. Las causas de muerte más habituales son la depredación, los atropellos y la caza furtiva.

La revisión llevada a cabo por Lozano (2017) refleja las siguientes presiones y amenazas:
a) La destrucción directa del hábitat, relacionada con la urbanización o la quema de matorral como medida preventiva para reducir la cantidad y virulencia de los incendios forestales.
B) El empleo de jaulas trampa para el control de depredadores en cotos de caza menor de conejo afecta de manera importante al gato montés, a pesar del aumento de conejos en zonas donde el gato montés también aumentó su abundancia (Lozano et al. 2005).
C) Uso ilegal de cebos envenenados, que matan de manera intencionada o accidental a numerosas especies, entre las que se encuentra el gato montés (cano et al. 2006)
d) Introgresión genética del gato doméstico. Este hecho se ha constatado en varios países europeos (Escocia y Hungría) en los que se considera un factor de presión y amenaza muy importante. En la península se ha constatado recientemente la introgresión genética en el 11-12% de los ejemplares en Portugal (Oliveira et al. 2008a, 2008b) y entre un 4% (mediante análisis genéticos) y un 20% (mediante caracteres craneométricos) en España. Estos valores seguirán aumentando a medida que los gatos domésticos y asilvestrados vayan colonizando el medio natural.
E) Los atropellos de gatos monteses son frecuentes en todas las provincias en las que se han llevado a cabo estudios.

pressures_info: Other IAS: las especies exóticas invasoras piscícolas como el lucio (Esox lucius) compiten y depredan sobre el barbo común. El visón americano (Neovison vison) depreda igualmente sobre el barbo común.
La especie podría estar siendo afectada actualmente por un hongo que provoca una podredumbre acuosa en los tejidos de la planta.

Díaz-Bertrana (2017) comenta que en la parte baja del barranco, cerca de donde se encuentra la población, se localiza una considerable infraestructura para la organización de las apañadas de ganado, al igual que unas mangas hechas con tela metálica.

Con respecto a la presión L06, es de destacar que, aunque las cabras ramonean ocasionalmente las plantas de esta planta, la principal afección por el pastoreo es la derivada del pisoteo, que genera erosión de las capas superficiales de suelo. Además, el ganado también ocasiona la destrucción del matorral, provocando cambios en la composición de la vegetación e impidiendo el avance de los núcleos poblacionales por destrucción de plántulas y juveniles.

pressures_info: Las plantas de flora introducidas en el islote de Alborán, así como la proliferación de gaviotas, afectan significativamente al hábitat adecuado para la especie.

Mediante la observación de las presiones y amenazas que existen en el hábitat y las parcelas de seguimiento y los efectos que produce en la especie objeto de estudio se ha detectado sobrepastoreo extensivo (especialmente vacuno) junto con la predación por parte de ungulados silvestres. Asimismo, se observa cierto pisoteo y rotura de escapos florales por parte de animales, aunque en algunos casos se debe a la acción de insectos que predan los tallos fistulosos. También se han observado numerosos individuos secos, debido a una eventual desecación por una disminución del caudal de los arroyos, ya sea por causas naturales o por el manejo hídrico de las acequias de careo típicas de Sierra Nevada. La proximidad a carriles y senderos transitados también puede suponer una potencial amenaza, así como la nitrificación puede promover la competencia con especies nitrófilas, mientras que la proliferación de Carex camposii por una disminución drástica de la herbivoría ha sido citada como una de las posibles causas de su retroceso.

Las presiones y amenazas para esta especie son de importancia baja (L): Modificación de prácticas agrícolas, pastoreo, abandono de sistemas de pastoreo o ausencia de pastoreo, repoblación e incendios y extinción de incendios.

pressures_info:
Les pressions et menaces impactant Caropsis verticillatinundata sont assez diversifiées, en fonction des sites et activités dominantes en périphérie.

On distingue des pressions et menaces par :
-  destruction  directe  : du fait d’opérations d’aménagements tels que  la  construction  de routes / pontons / accès plage et rives,  de  la  disparition des zones humides (mise en culture (agricole ou sylvicole) de zones humides, comblement, effacement, etc.) .

- destructions indirectes 
   * Sur les rives des  étangs arrière-littoraux girondins et  landais, le maintien d’un niveau d’eau élevé durant la période estivale perturbe le cycle biologique de l’espèce, l’absence de période d’exondation empêchant la formation des ombelles.
   * l’abandon  progressif  des  activités  agricoles  traditionnelles  en rapport  avec  les  zones  humides  (pâturage, fauche, extraction de la tourbe) conduit à la disparition des habitats pionniers favorables au Faux cresson de Thore. Le milieu se ferme et cette dynamique s’accompagne d’une surélévation du  niveau du sol (atterrissement).
   * colonisation par des espèces envahissantes, en particulier la Jussie à grandes fleurs (Ludwigia grandiflora)

pressures_source: Paill (2005), Wurst & Klausnitzer (2003)

pressures_info:
A21 B. barbastellus also forages in small-scale agrarian landscape (bocage). Use of pesticides has a negative impact on prey availability (moths are the main prey group)
A05 Important for foraging and connectivity (commuting flights)
B02 B. barbastellus depends on structure-rich forests with a well developed undergrowth (Russo et al. 2004). Pressure H, but threat M since actual forest management takes this aspect into account.
B06 & B09 B. barbastellus roosts and hibernates mostly in trees and needs a high density of potential tree roosts in the summer (Hillen et al. 2010, Russo et al. 2004). The actual trend to remove non-native trees can lead to an additional rarefaction of potential roosts.
B07 Loss of roosts and hibernation sites in trees. Loose bark of dead trees is often used as summer roost (Russo et al. 2004)
F02 Several of the few known summer roosts in Flanders are behind window shutters
F24 B. barbastellus also roosts in buildings and is sensitive to light (Voigt et al. 2018)
E01 B. barbastellus is sensitive to light (Voigt et al. 2018) but still has been shown to be able to cross roads (Kerth & Melber 2009, Everaert pers. com.) so we chose a score M.
F07 B. barbastellus is very sensitive to disturbance. Human presence near roosts can put the bats to flight (Russo et al. 2004). Recreational cave visits are also unfavourable since B. barbastellus occasionally hibernates in underground sites.

Other IAS: Las especies que se mencionan a continuación, son especies introducidas de manera ilegal que están generando graves problemas a la fauna piscícola autóctona, ya que la mayoría no tienen depredadores y además su tasa de reproducción es muy alta. Dichas especies son: carpa (Cyprinus carpio), lucio (Esox lucius), blackbass o perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), salmón del Danubio (Hucho hucho), gambusia (Gambusia holbrooki), perca-sol (Lepomis gibbosus), trucha arcoiris (Oncothynchus mykiss), pez gato negro (Ameiurus melas) y siluro (Silurus glanis).

Other IAS: Un importante factor de amenaza es la introducción de especies exóticas, que bien actúan directamente como competidoras o indirectamente provocando una disminución en la disponibilidad de hábitat para la especie. Estas especies exóticas son fundamentalmente el pez sol (Lepomis gibbosus), la perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), la gambusia (Gambusia holbrooki), el fúndulo (Fundulus heteroclitus) o la carpa (Cyprinus carpio) (Doadrio, 2001; Torralva et al., 2001; Navarro et al., 2017; Zamora et al., 2017).

pressures_info: Otras presiones y amenazas de importancia baja (L) a tener en cuenta son: los incendios y extinción de incendios y la recolección y eliminación de plantas terrestres en general.
El desplazamiento de la especie por competencia interespecífica con los retamares (Retama monosperma), se considera la más importante de las presiones actuales que inciden sobre esta especie.

Otras amenazas a tener en cuenta son la disminución o pérdida de las características específicas de un hábitat (M), la disminución de la conectividad de hábitats debida a causas antropogénicas (M), los cambios en la composición de las especies (sucesiones)(M), los cambios térmicos (M), la sequía y disminución  de la precipitación (M) y cambios y alteraciones de hábitat (M).

pressures_info: The pressure A09 - Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock applies only for the subpopulation in the Akamas peninsula. Also, the threat E01 - Roads, paths, railroads and related infrastructure (e.g. bridges, viaducts, tunnels) applies only for a few locations which are outside the State Forest land.

Climate change may affect the species in the long term (i.e. the next 20 and 50 years). No short - or medium - term impacts are expected. In the long term, the species may be affected by the reduction of precipitation (especially in the winter months when germination processes take place) and the desynchronisation of processes (the flowering of other species on the same period as Crocus cyprius, which is currently flowering in late winter/early spring, may hinder insect pollination).
The species may also be impacted  by temperature rise (impact on germination conditions) and migration of other species.

Due to lack of a more suitable code in the Pressure/Threat code list on the Reference Portal, code J01 was used to also describe the insect (mainly mosquito) control practices (sprayings) carried out near torrents, streams and other water bodies.

pressures_info:
La principal alteración tiene que ver con el efecto del ganado sobre la vegetación de montaña. En general la presencia de herbívoros asegura el mantenimiento de pastos y claros en la vegetación arborea que permiten la colonización y asentamiento de poblaciones de esta especie. La supresión del pastoreo puede conllevar la desaparición de estas poblaciones, sin embargo un exceso de cabaña ganadera puede también ser fuente de amenaza por alteración del hábitat, pisoteo y predación directa sobre flores principalmente.
Otras amenazas señaladas tienen que ver con la recolección en ciertas partes de la cordillera Cantábrica y Pirineos y en el País Vasco. La creacion de infraestructuras de montaña, pistas, carreteras, instalaciones para la práctica del esquí o la canalización de fuentes son amenazas que pueden influir a escala local a la supervivencia de la especie.
Las especies exóticas invasoras son todas las especies del género Eucalyptus

pressures_info: Durante el año 2000 se comprobó que el Parvovirus de la Enfermedad Aleutiana (ADV), es una enfermedad que afectaba a un alto porcentaje de la población de visón europeo que, según zonas, va desde un 33% (Navarra y Álava) de los ejemplares analizados, a más de los 65% (La Rioja y Burgos) (Palazón, 2017). Sin embargo, estudios recientes restan importancia a tal enfermedad ya que las poblaciones sanas de visón europeo poseen resistencia natural al patógeno y por tanto éste no supone una amenaza letal para la supervivencia de la especie, si bien otra amenaza potencial (Mañas, 2016).
pressures_source: La expansión del visón americano (Neovison vison) en el área de distribución del visón europeo ha sido la principal presión para la especie. El área de distribución del visón americano en España se duplicó cada 5 años entre 1985 y 2012. En 2012, más de ¼ del país estaba colonizado y la especie americana había invadido 1/3 del área histórica del visón europeo (Põdra & Gómez, 2018). El elevado número capturas de visón americano entre 2013-18 ha permitido reducir esa presión, pero la amenaza se sigue considerando alta por la migración que se produce desde la cuenca del Duero (meseta norte, provincias Burgos y Soria) y por distintos tramos ocupados en la cuenca alta del Ebro: tanto desde el tramo alto (Cantabria y Burgos) como desde el tramo bajo (Aragón, Cataluña) y el esfuerzo de control a realizar en los próximos años en esos entornos podría no ser suficiente para su expansión a otras zonas.

pressures_info: Otras amenazas a tener en cuenta son los vehículos motorizados (M) y deportes al aire libre y actividades de ocio (M), avistamiento de animales (M), pisoteo y uso excesivo (M), medidas de conservación inapropiadas o ausentes (M), quemas intencionadas (M), disminución de la pérdida de las características específicas de un hábitat (M), cambios en la composición de especies (sucesiones)(M), competencia (M), cambios térmicos (M), sequía y disminución de la precipitación (M), mejora de accesos (M) e incendios naturales (M).

pressures_source: La mayor parte de las presiones y amenazas provienen de la gestión y el manejo forestal. La falta de árboles maduros y viejos y de gran porte, de pies muertos y de un sotobosque abundante, limita los refugios y el hábitat necesario para la reproducción de esta especie, estrictamente forestal (Coronado et al., 2017) (https://doi.org/10.4404/HYSTRIX-00004-2017).

pressures_info: La introducción de especies exóticas en su área de ocupación como son son el pez sol (Lepomis gibbosus) , el pez gato (Ameiurus melas), perca americana  (Micropterus salmoides), lucio (Esox lucius), lucioperca (Stizostedion lucioperca) y diversas especies de ciprínidos es una de las principales causas del declive de esta especie.

pressures_info: Díaz-Bertrana (2016) y Mesa Coello (2013) afirman que en los riscos existen rezumaderos y nacientes de agua que se siguen aprovechando, por lo que cada cierto tiempo se acondicionan senderos hasta la zona de 'Caideros Altos', desbrozando las especies del entorno; se observan varios ejemplares de Bencomia brachystachya cortados por su base. Por otra parte, puede que la causa de la ausencia de regeneración sea la competencia que ejerce la vegetación acompañante con un denso matorral.

La presión y amenaza indicada como (L06), incluye varias presiones a las que se ve sometida la especie; destrucción de juveniles y plántulas por conejos, destrucción de semillas por larvas de coleópteros (Bruchidius lichenicola) y también la competencia vegetal natural, que impide la floración normal de los ejemplares en los que se cierra el dosel del manto (TRAGSATEC, 2015).
Delgado Rodríguez & Mesa Coello (2006) mencionan el arranque o corte de ejemplares en las labores de limpieza del canal en Barranco del Agua.
TRAGSATEC (2015) indica que como hecho puntual, la subpoblación de la localidad 'Barranco del agua' se vio afectada por la erosión ocasionada por las lluvias torrenciales de 2005, que destruyó una decena de plantas.
Other IAS: el conejo (Oryctolagus cuniculus) ejerce presión por hervivoría sobre esta especie, no es una especie autóctona.
Rodríguez & Mesa (2006) concluyen que el principal factor de amenaza que pesa sobre la especie es la depredación por herbívoros alóctonos (conejos y muflones).

pressures_info: Otra amenaza a tener en cuenta es la sequía y disminución de la precipitación (M).

Otras presiones a tener en cuenta pero de importancia baja (L) son la depredación, el pastoreo no intensivo de ovejas y cabras, las sacas no intensivas (dejando madera muerta/árboles viejos intactos), excursionismo, equitación y uso de vehículos no motorizados, saños causados por herbívoros (incluyendo especies de caza) y disminución de la fecundidad o disminución de la variabilidad genética en plantas (incluyendo endogamia).

Otras amenazas a tener en cuenta pero de importancia baja (L) son la depredación, el aumento de la temperatura y temperaturas extremas, disminución de la fecundidad/disminución de la variabilidad genética en plantas (incluye endogamia), incendios, circuitos y pistas y sequía y disminución de la precipitación.

La lagartija balear (Podarcis lilfordi), es su único dispersor conocido, y actualmente la interacción entre la lagartija y la planta solamente se encuentra en el islote de Colom, ya que Podarcis lilfordi está extinta en la isla de Menorca, y la planta carece de dispersores en la isla mayor (Rodríguez-Perez & Traveset, 2006).
Other IAS: especies de plantas exóticas muy invasivas de zonas costeras como la uña de gato (Carpobrotus edulis).
pressures_source: Uno de los principales problemas a los que se enfrenta la especie es la baja fecundidad y la pobreza genética de sus poblaciones (https://www.miteco.gob.es/es/biodiversidad/temas/inventarios-nacionales/1226_tcm30-99516.pdf).

pressures_info: Other IAS: la especie coexiste con dos especies exóticas invasoras , la gambusia (Gambusia holbrooki) y el fúndulo (Fundulus heteroclitus). Cuando esto sucede, el salinete es desplazado por competencia a aguas más saladas al convivir con la gambusia, y sufre la competencia del fúndulo en los hábitats más salobres. Otras especies exóticas invasoras como la perca americana (Micropterus salmoides) o el pez sol (Lepomis gibbosus) depredan directamente sobre el salinete (Doadrio et al., 2002; Clavero et al., 2004; Clavero et al., 2007).
pressures_source: Las poblaciones están muy amenazadas por la transformación del hábitat por actividades agrarias, la extracción del agua para campos de golf y las urbanizaciones costeras (Doadrio et al., 2011) (https://www.miteco.gob.es/es/biodiversidad/temas/inventarios-nacionales/03_ictiofauna_tcm30-208102.pdf).

pressures_info:
Pressures were listed, using field reports from the species monitoring in Latvia from 2013-2018, cross-reference from the previous species report from 2007-2012, and based on expert opinion.
In the field reports from Dytiscus latissimus monitoring in Latvia, water pollution was visually present in multiple localities where the species was or wasn’t abundant. In Latvia, the main water pollution is diffuse water pollution from agriculture and forestry activities. 
It is difficult to distinguish whether eutrophication occurs naturally or is caused by diffused pollution with nutrients, or both factors complement each other. 
Because of the eutrophication processes the expansion of common reed Phragmites australis also creates pressure and threat to Dytiscus latissimus, as it decreases the area of suitable microhabitat for the species. Cybister lateralimarginalis is a common native diving beetle species in Latvia, that was present in most of the water bodies, where Dytiscus latissimus was monitored. C. lateralimarginalis was often present in high abundance, suggesting possible resource competition with D. latissimus. 
According to the report of freshwater fish monitoring in Latvia (2013) Perccottus glenii (R11034) has been recorded in few water bodies – shallow, oxygen poor lakes, but the biomass and distribution of this fish species is low, and could only influence diving beetles locally, under specific environmental conditions. Therefore, in the reporting period, the pressure is considered low to medium, but if Perccottus glenii expands, it will create future threats to Dytiscus latissimus.

The most important ecological requirements are: high CO2 concentration in the water (>0.1 mM), shallow water of at least 0.1 m, habitats free of dense stands of either submerged, free-floating or emergent plants or filamentous algae, a thick layer of slowly decomposable plant litter on the bottom and abundant zooplankton as prey. Lycaena helle was quite common over most of northern and central Scandinavia as long as agricultural activities were relatively extensive. However, the population has declined during the second half of the 20th century. Previously suitable habitats have been abandoned and overgrown after cessation of grazing or of mowing as a result of abandonment of farming. Today the species survive better on limestone influenced and flower-rich wetlands with sparse vegetation than on farmland. The species also occurs in other disturbed "human" habitats like roadsides.
Future threats are similar to the present pressures.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

Biogeographical monitoring. Species is only found in not regulated rivers.
The unregulated rivers where it occurs are protected from being used for hydropower by law. A conservation effort. Thus, no direct conservations needs to be listed in section 9.

pressures_info: The greatest threat/pressure to the species is primarily modern forestry, involving the clearing of old growth forest. However, the species can be found for a limited time in clearings where aspen has been retained as general environmental consideration. Intense forest management focused on coniferous tree production gradually causes a deterioration of that environment and usually prevents the creation of new habitats. Firewood cutting and use of aspen wood for energy production (wood chips) are an increasing threats. Badly designed restoration of wooded pastures can also result in poorer habitats. Regulation of the river Dalälven has led to aspen-rich floodplain forest being invaded by Norway spruce Picea abies. Furthermore, beaver Castor fiber, elk Alces alces and roe deer Capreolus capreolus, grazers of aspen, may locally become a threat to the species.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info: The habitat of the species is spruce swamp forests with a long continuity of spruce trees. The spruce forest should be more than 170 years old and contain a high volume dead wood. Today, an extremely rare habitat in the Swedish forest landscape due to extensive logging. P. kolwensis is probably intolerant of practically any tree removal reducing the recruitment rate of new host trees in its habitats. Modification of hydrological conditions in forestry is also a problem, including maintainance of old draining ditches. The species is therefore mainly dependent on forest reserves for its survival.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info:
Las principales presiones y amenazas para la especie son compartidas con la otra especie del género Eptesicus (Eptesicus isabellinus). En ambas especies están relacionadas con el cerramiento inadecuado de edificaciones, la destrucción de los refugios en zonas urbanas, así como la fragmentación del hábitat y el uso de plaguicidas, que indicen sobre los insectos, sus presas principales (Lisón, 2017).

Se considera también que el sellado inadecuado de las juntas de dilatación de puentes y otras infraestructuras utilizadas como refugio por esta especie puede llevar a la desaparición de ciertas colonias (Ibáñez, 2007).

Sería necesario evaluar la importancia de los parques eólicos en la mortalidad de la especie, como se ha hecho con otras, ya que es habitual que su área de campeo incluya zonas abiertas por encima del dosel arbóreo, a la altura de las aspas (Camina 2012). En el estudio desarrollado por Alcalde (2003) se encontraron varios ejemplares de esta especie en instalaciones eólicas localizadas en Navarra.

pressures_info:
El abandono ganadero en las turberas ha propiciado el embastecimiento de la vegetación en algunos enclaves. Este proceso va cerrando los ambientes de turbera dejando sin nicho a las especies más heliófilas, típicas de estos ecosistemas. En la Sierra de Xistral, donde se concentra la población actual, la presión ganadera parece, en términos generales, más acorde con la conservación de estos hábitats.
Las canalizaciones y soterramiento de regatos impiden la infiltración del agua, los drenajes para ganar terreno de pastizal acaban con los prados húmedos y zonas encharcadizas que quedan relegadas a pequeños fragmentos entre las lindes de los prados.
Las explotaciones forestales (pinos y eucaliptos) son numerosas en la zona prospectada y, aún en los casos en los que no ocupan directamente el terreno de las turberas, sino solo  sus alrededores, pueden igualmente reducir el aporte de agua que les llega del subsuelo.
Las localidades de la Sierra de Xistral están ocupadas en la actualidad por parques eólicos que potencialmente pueden tener efecto sobre las turberas de la zona, tanto por alteración de las escorrentías como por el aporte de los materiales de construcción de calcio y minerales a estos enclaves ácidos y poco o nada minerotróficos (Heras, 2005).
pressures_source: Seguimiento para CEDEX (Martínez, 2018).

pressures_info: La planta está estrechamente ligada a hábitats localmente raros, sensibles y de reducida superficie, por lo que las principales amenazas de la especie están directamente relacionadas con los medios húmedos de que dependen. Las captaciones de agua, el pastoreo o, en ocasiones, el abandono del mismo, así como las obras de acondicionamiento y mejora de carreteras y pistas próximas a las poblaciones son los principales factores de riesgo.

pressures_info:
Las principales presiones y amenazas para la especie son compartidas con la otra especie del género Eptesicus (Eptesicus isabellinus). Para ambas especies, las presiones y amenazas están relacionadas con el cerramiento inadecuado de edificaciones, la destrucción de los refugios en zonas urbanas, así como la fragmentación del hábitat y el uso de plaguicidas, que indicen sobre los insectos, sus presas principales (Lisón, 2017).

Se considera también que el sellado inadecuado de las juntas de dilatación de puentes y otras infraestructuras utilizadas como refugio por esta especie puede llevar a la desaparición de ciertas colonias (Ibáñez, 2007).

Sería necesario evaluar la importancia de los parques eólicos en la mortalidad de la especie, como se ha hecho con otras, ya que es habitual que su área de campeo incluya zonas abiertas por encima del dosel arbóreo, a la altura de las aspas (Camina 2012). En el estudio desarrollado por Alcalde (2003) se encontraron varios ejemplares de esta especie en instalaciones eólicas localizadas en Navarra.

La destrucción y ruina de las edificaciones antiguas está afectando negativamente a la especie, así como el cambio en los usos del suelo y la deforestación. La existencia de líneas de vegetación que aporten conectividad al paisaje resulta de gran importancia para esta especie. Esta conectividad es especialmente pobre en los páramos cerealistas, en los que las líneas de vegetación a menudo se limitan al cauce del río (Lisón et al., 2010; Hermida et al., 2018).

pressures_info: La principal amenaza consiste en la alteración de la composición química del sustrato debido al abandono de las prácticas agrarias tradicionales o el uso de prácticas agrarias inadecuadas.

Otras amenazas a tener en cuenta son los cultivos (M), el abandono/ausencia de la siega (M), el pastoreo intensivo (M), la ausencia o disminución de polinizadores (M),los daños causados por herbívoros (incluyendo especies de caza (M), la sequía y disminución de la precipitación (M) y cambios y alteraciones de hábitat (M).
Otras amenazas y presiones a tener en cuenta son la prospección y extracción de petróleo o gas natural, la sequía y la disminución de la precipitación.

pressures_info:
Les menaces/pressions diffèrent en fonction des populations. 
Les populations arrière-littorales se distinguent en particulier par une menace directe du fait de l'érosion marine et de la disparition / assèchement / saupoudrage éolien des pannes arrière-dunaires qui abritaient l'espèce, sans reformation de surfaces d'habitats favorables en retrait du littoral.
La majorité des populations subissent des pressions / menaces du fait de la fermeture naturelle (succession naturelle), mais aussi de régimes hydrologiques défavorables, parfois sur plusieurs années successives, pouvant être pour partie en lien avec des activités agricoles, de carrières ou la proximité de zones résidentielles.

pressures_info: La pression " C01-Exploitations minières (par ex. roche, minerais métalliques, gravier, sable, coquillages) ", si elle présente d'une part une menace potentielle de par l'activité générant des risques de perturbation des milieux et de destruction directe des individus et des larves, peut être compensée par un engagement contractuel des exploitants de carrières à effectuer pendant la durée de l'exploitation des suivis annuels par des écologues et à suivre les prescriptions destinées à éviter les effets négatifs, voire à augmenter les capacités d'accueil des milieux. Ce dispositif, encadré par la DREAL Grand Est et figurant dans les arrêtés préfectoraux autorisant l'exploitation, a permis au cours de la période de référence à court terme, de maintenir en bon état de conservation et à développer des populations de Crapaud vert présentes sur les sites d'extraction, notamment dans le département du Bas-Rhin.

pressures_source:
Esta especie es muy sensible ante la presencia de peces exóticos introducidos y el cangrejo rojo americano (Procambarus clarkii) en su fase acuática (Montori, 2010; Pleguezuelos et al., 2002).
Hay una elevada tasa de mortalidad debido al tráfico en carreteras durante la migración reproductora y los movimientos migratorios (Montori, 2014).
Algunas especies exóticas invasoras son un vector de enfermedades infecciosas muy perjudiciales para los anfibios, como el ranavirus o el hongo quitridio (Palomar et al., 2016; Miller et al., 2011; Pounds et al., 2006; Bosch et al., 2001, 2006).

Después de la introducción de especies exóticas invasoras como el cangrejo rojo americano (Procambarus clarkii) o la gambusia (Gambusia holbrooki) en los embalses de Vilasenín y Meicende, las poblaciones de Alytes obstetricans sufrieron declives o la extinción de sus poblaciones (Galán, 2008).
Algunas especies invasoras introducidas son el vector de enfermedades muy perjudiciales para los anfibios, como Ranavirus y el hongo quitridio (Palomar et al., 2016; Miller et al., 2011; Pounds et al., 2006; Bosch et al., 2001, 2006).

pressures_info:
Uno de los mayores problemas a los que se enfrenta el lobo ibérico es la baja variabilidad genética de sus efectivos poblacionales. Durante los años 70 del siglo XX estuvo cerca de la extinción por lo que pudo haberse producido un "cuello de botella" genético del que no se ha recuperado y que pone en peligro la viabilidad de la población ibérica a largo plazo. Sería necesario generar flujos continuos de efectivos entre la Península Ibérica y Centro-Europa a través de corredores ecológicos, para evitar este aislamiento genético (Sastre et al., 2010; Pilot et al., 2014; Hindrikson et al., 2017; ASCEL, 2018).
Se ha demostrado la hibridación de lobos con perros asilvestrados y perros domésticos en ambientes antropizados en los límites de su área de distribución (Godinho et al., 2011; Leonard, 2014; Pacheco et al., 2017).
pressures_source: Durante el período 2001 - 2016 las causas de muertes no naturales de lobo ibérico registradas son en un 70% provocadas por los controles poblacionales realizados por las administraciones, seguidas de un 12% por atropellos, un 12% son muertes indeterminadas, un 5% por disparos de caza y lazos (incluyendo furtivismo) y un 2% por veneno y tóxicos. En 2017, se estiman entre 500-650 lobos muertos por las causas anteriores en toda España, cifra que se considera próxima o igual a la tasa de reclutamiento de la especie, lo cual incide severamente en su recuperación y estado de conservación (Sánchez et al., 2017) (https://censoloboiberico.files.wordpress.com/2018/03/informe-mortandad-lobo-iberico-20171.pdf).

pressures_info:
La principal alteración tiene que ver con el efecto del ganado sobre la vegetación de montaña. En general la presencia de herbívoros asegura el mantenimiento de pastos y claros en la vegetación arborea que permiten la colonización y asentamiento de poblaciones de esta especie. La supresión del pastoreo puede conllevar la desaparición de estas poblaciones, sin embargo un exceso de cabaña ganadera puede también ser fuente de amenaza por alteración del hábitat, pisoteo y predación directa sobre flores principalmente.
Otras amenazas señaladas tienen que ver con la recolección en ciertas partes de la cordillera Cantábrica y Pirineos y en el País Vasco. La creacion de infraestructuras de montaña, pistas, carreteras, instalaciones para la práctica del esquí o la canalización de fuentes son amenazas que pueden influir a escala local a la supervivencia de la especie.
Otras amenazas a tener en cuenta son los cambios en la composición de las especies (sucesiones)(M) y la sequía y disminución de la precipitación (M).

I02- Other IAS: la introducción de especies exóticas en su área de ocupación como son el pez sol (Lepomis gibbosus), el pez gato (Ameiurus melas), perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), lucio (Esox lucius), lucioperca (Stizostedion lucioperca) y diversas especies de ciprínidos es una de las principales causas del declive de esta especie.

Dichas especies son: carpa (Cyprinus carpio), lucio (Esox lucius), blackbass o perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), salmón del Danubio (Hucho hucho), gambusia (Gambusia holbrooki), perca-sol (Lepomis gibbosus), trucha arcoiris (Oncothynchus mykiss), pez gato negro (Ameiurus melas) y siluro (silurus glanis).

El estado de regresión de Myrica rivas-martinezii viene determinado por su carácter
dióico y por el grado de aislamiento existente entre individuos, así como por la capacidad de hibridación con Myrica faya. Debe destacarse que algunos autores apuntan a que Myrica rivas-martinezii podría no tener entidad como especie (según bibliografía sobre estudios genéticos).

'L06': esta presión engloba, tanto a la competencia vegetal natural que soporta la especie, como a la hibridación. A este respecto, Mesa & Paredes (2013) señalan que, al disminuir el pastoreo, ha proliferado el matorral competidor, observándose en algunas zonas una proliferación notable del pajonasco (Brachypodium arbuscula) que ha desplazado a Sideritis marmorea en áreas puntuales. La hibridación natural con Sideritis spicata ocurre de manera puntual en la parte inferior del areal de distribución al final de la localidad de 'Frontón de Puntallana'.

pressures_source: Las plantaciones de monocultivos de eucalipto (Eucaliptus spp) han reducido o totalmente hecho desaparecer varias poblaciones de reptiles y anfibios en el Noroeste peninsular (Arntzen, 2015; Galán, 2005). La contaminación de ríos y arroyos y la fragmentación del hábitat fluvial provocado por presas hidroeléctricas y azudes también suponen una gran afección para esta especie (http://www.lacerta.de/AS/Bibliografie/BIB_12517.pdf) (Galán, P. 2005).

pressures_source:
Esta especie es muy sensible ante la presencia de peces exóticos introducidos y el cangrejo rojo americano (Procambarus clarkii) en su fase acuática (Montori, 2010; Pleguezuelos et al., 2002).
Hay una elevada tasa de mortalidad debido al tráfico en carreteras durante la migración reproductora y los movimientos migratorios (Montori, 2014).
Algunas especies exóticas invasoras son un vector de enfermedades infecciosas muy perjudiciales para los anfibios, como el ranavirus o el hongo quitridio (Palomar et al., 2016; Miller et al., 2011; Pounds et al., 2006; Bosch et al., 2001, 2006).

En un escenario futuro de cambio climático y aridificación/desertificación del hábitat disponible, se espera una reducción de las localidades donde habita Algyroides marchi dado su carácter de especie estenotópica (Rubio, 2002; Pérez-Mellado et al., 2008; Rubio & Martín, 2017).
Un factor de amenaza potencial son las infraestructuras hidrológicas, como las del río Castril (Rubio et al., 1998; Fernández-Cardenete et al., 2000; González de la Vega et al., 2001; Rubio, 2002).
En las áreas de borde donde se localiza la especie, se han detectado alteraciones tales como construcción de carreteras y pistas forestales, urbanización y presión ganadera (Rubio & Martín, 2017).

Especies introducidas en las Islas Baleares (culebras, mamíferos, ratas) representan un factor actual de presión y amenaza que dificultan la recolonización de la lagartija limitando sus poblaciones a los islotes costeros. Otra presión de entidad son los animales domésticos asilvestrados, especialmente las cabras y los gatos (Salvador 2015; Pérez-Mellado et al., 1997, 2008).

pressures_info:
La especie se ve principalmente afectada por la destrucción de las masas forestales, sobre todo las más maduras, que suelen proporcionar oquedades donde se instalan las colonias de cría. También el manejo inapropiado de los hábitats forestales genera molestias y destrucción del hábitat adecuado.
La agricultura intensiva junto con el uso de pesticidas y las molestias en los refugios como cuevas y grutas constituyen otros factores de amenaza.

Otras presiones a tener en cuenta son otras actividades agrícolas no mencionadas anteriormente (M), captaciones de agua proveniente de aguas superficiales (H) y de agua subterránea (H), eutrofización natural (H), sequía y disminución de la precipitación (H), conversión de la tierra de un tipo de uso agrícola a otro (H), prácticas de labranza (H), regadío (M), Eliminación de pequeñas características del paisaje para la consolidación de parcelas de tierras agrícolas (M), modificación del flujo hidrológico (H) y procesos abióticos naturales (M).

Las principales presiones y amenazas de esta especie son de importancia baja (L): cortas a hecho, desecación, incendios naturales y pastoreo. Como amenazas además destacan los incendios y su extinción y la disminución de la fecundidad/variabilidad genética en plantas (incluye endogamia).
pressures_source: Opinión experta.

pressures_info:
A partir de 1980 se ha observado la creciente utilización del islote por parte de gaviotas
(Larus michahellis), que lo usan como reposadero y nidificación, con el consiguiente pisoteo del reducido espacio disponible para el asentamiento de pies jóvenes de la planta.
La principal presión y amenaza de esta especie es la contaminación genética aunque es de importancia baja (L).

pressures_info:
There were often more than ten pressures or threats (of high or medium importance) identified, and an aggregation method was used to identify the top ten of each. As a result the top ten lists for the feature may not correspond with each other. For example, a pressure may be in the reported top ten list, but may not appear in the top ten list of threats. This does not necessarily mean that the threat was not listed but instead it is in the extended list of threats that did not make the top ten but are detailed in the additional information section.
The following pressures were also identified, however, a maximum of 10 could be listed: D05- Development and operation of energy production plants (including bioenergy plants, fossil and nuclear energy plants), E07- Land, water and air transport activities generating marine pollution, F07- Sports, tourism and leisure activities, F20- Residential or recreational activities and structures generating marine pollution (excl. marine macro- and micro-particular pollution)
The following threats were also identified, however, a maximum of 10 could be listed: D05- Development and operation of energy production plants (including bioenergy plants, fossil and nuclear energy plants), E07- Land, water and air transport activities generating marine pollution, F07- Sports, tourism and leisure activities, F20- Residential or recreational activities and structures generating marine pollution (excl. marine macro- and micro-particular pollution), F21- Industrial or commercial activities and structures generating marine pollution (excluding marine macro- and micro-particular pollution), A28- Agricultural activities generating marine pollution, C01- Extraction of minerals (e.g. rock, metal ores, gravel, sand, shell), C07- Dumping/depositing of dredged materials from marine extraction, D01- Wind, wave and tidal power, including infrastructure, E02- Shipping lanes and ferry lanes transport operations, J02- Mixed source marine water pollution (marine and coastal)

pressures_info:
"The code Xo ‘Threats and pressures from outside the Member State’ is used for '% of the rough nitrogen deposition from outside the Flanders region’. An important fraction of this foreign deposition consists of NH3, which is almost entirely attributable to agriculture. Nevertheless A27 ‘Agricultural activities generating air pollution’ is strictly referring to deposition originating from within Flanders. If one would combine air pollution generated by agricultural activities from inside and outside the Flanders region and abroad, then the importance of A27 may rank higher.
For other pollutants or human-induced changes in water regimes we lack data to link these with key sectoral driver(s). In many cases these are related to several causes. As such these are reported under the categories J and K."

pressures_info: I02 has not been qualified for the top 10 pressures/threats for this habitat type. However, Navarra region has reported the following invasive species as a pressure/threat: Ailanthus altissima and Robinia pseudoacacia.

The pressures detected and communicated in previous sexennial reports are becoming less relevant, due to the application of the Natura 2000 sites management plans,  in which this hábitat is mostly located. Therefore, and since the category of less importance (L) has been deleted, it is considered that there are no pressures noteworthy. In relation to the threats, the main ones for this habitat are those that appear in any of the predicted climate change scenarios (see http://climaimpacto.eu/efectos/escenarios-climaticos-en-canarias/), although the uncertainty is still too high to be considered as threats of medium importance.

On the other hand, the existing fire extinguishing system makes fire (codes A11, H04 and M09) to be considered a threat of only minor importance

Other IAS: Agave americana, Carpobrotus edulis, Opuntia maxima, Opuntia ficus-indica, Carpobrotus chilensis, Mesembryanthemum crystallinum, Amaranthus blitoides, Amaranthus muricatus, Ricinus communis, Oxalis pes-caprae, Aster squamatus & Nicotiana glauca

pressures_info:
Free et al. (2009) recognised that the main threats to isoetid lakes across Europe come from eutrophication, acidification and alkalinisation, water level changes, habitat destruction, peat erosion and invasive alien species.
Pressures and threats reported for the 2007-2012 cycle were used again for this cycle. NPWS (2013) detailed how these were identified through examination of information on pressures on general water quality and use of expert judgement. See also O Connor (2015).
In addition, reference was made to the 2018-2021 River Basin Management Plan under the Water Framework Directive, which included an assessment of significant pressures impacting on water status for the 16% of lake water bodies, or 132 individual lakes, considered to be ‘at risk’ (Government of Ireland, 2018). It was not possible to readily access the pressures and impacts data for specific lakes, so analyses could not be conducted to distinguish the pressures impacting on 3110. Agriculture was identified as a significant pressure at 80 lakes, or 61% of ‘at risk’ lakes (Government of Ireland, 2018). The other significant pressures identified as impacting on the ‘at risk’ lakes were forestry at 18 lakes (14%), urban and domestic waste-water at 15 lakes each (11%), hydromorphology at 10 lakes (8%), industry and peat extraction each at 3 lakes (2%) and urban run-off at 2 lakes (2%) (Government of Ireland, 2018).
Pollution with dissolved and particulate organic matter and nutrients, as well as fine sediment and increased water colour, are significant concerns for habitat 3110. Pollution can arise from agriculture, primarily through land reclamation and fertilisation practices, as well as over-grazing of peatland. Increased stocking densities and housing of stock, leading to the generation of slurry, are particular concerns. Code A26 for diffuse pollution was used, although, with drainage of peatland and wet soils in these catchments, agricultural pollution can effectively be point source. Pollution also arises from forestry (B23), where slow decomposition of organic soils, conifer needles and small branches leads to significant losses of organic matter and, ultimately, nutrients. Similar types of pollution, albeit smaller loads, result from turf cutting.
Peatland and other organic soils are drained in 3110 catchments to facilitate agriculture, conifer plantations and peat extraction. Drains can be pollution sources, as well as impacting the hydrological regimes of lakes (A31, B27 and C05).
F12 covers discharges from both urban waste-water (UWW) treatment plants (sewered) and on-site systems (unsewered). On-site systems are more common pressures in the catchments of 3110 lakes.
Owing to their likely continuation, all pressures were also listed as threats.
No genuine changes in pressures or threats were identified. Both the reporting form and Article 17 pressures and threats list have changed since Article 17 2013.
Abstractions for drinking water (F33) or other purposes (F34, A30, C14) are considered to be a pressures of low importance, as they impact on relatively few 3110 lakes. It is possible, however, that abstractions may be a more significant threat, in combination with predicted droughts owing to climate change.

pressures_info:
Pressures were listed, using field reports from the species monitoring in Latvia from 2013-2018, cross-reference from the previous species report from 2007-2012, and based on expert opinion.
In the field reports from Graphoderus bilineatus monitoring in Latvia, water pollution was visually present in multiple localities where the species was or wasn’t abundant. In Latvia, the main water pollution is diffuse water pollution from agriculture and forestry activities. 
It is difficult to distinguish whether eutrophication occurs naturally or is caused by diffused pollution with nutrients, or both factors complement each other. 
Because of the eutrophication processes the expansion of common reed Phragmites australis also creates pressure and threat to Graphoderus bilineatus, as it decreases the area of suitable microhabitat for the species. Cybister lateralimarginalis is a common native diving beetle species in Latvia, that was present in most of the water bodies, where Graphoderus bilineatus was monitored. C. lateralimarginalis was often present in high abundance, suggesting possible resource competition with D. latissimus. 
According to the report of freshwater fish monitoring in Latvia (2013) Perccottus glenii (R11034) has been recorded in few water bodies – shallow, oxygen poor lakes, but the biomass and distribution of this fish species is low, and could only influence diving beetles locally, under specific environmental conditions. Therefore, in the reporting period, the pressure is considered low to medium, but if Perccottus glenii expands, it will create future threats to Graphoderus bilineatus.

pressures_info:
Red corals have been subject to heavy exploitation throughout the Mediterranean (Cattaneo-Vietti et al., 2016) for the jewellery trade, which led to the depletion of shallow water populations that were more accessible (Garrabou et al., 2017; Jaziri et al., 2017). Besides legally permitted exploitation in some countries (e.g. Tunisia), there is the threat of illegal fishing for these corals (Cattaneo-Vietti et al., 2017). Therefore, distribution maps, colony sizes and densities, as discovered through the LIFE Ba?AR for N2K project, should be treated as “sensitive” (confidential) information. This will help safeguard against attempts for illegal harvesting of the red coral.

The main pressure observed through the LIFE Ba?AR for N2K project is that of marine litter. This includes the presence of abandoned, lost or otherwise discarded fishing gear (ALDFG) such as fishing lines, which can get entangled in corals. In addition, as deep water technologies become more accessible, there is also the threat of exploitation.

Cependant, la multiplication des "petits" travaux en rivière (restauration des berges, aménagement de franchissement, reméandrage, etc.) est susceptible d'impacter fortement l'habitat du Desman et de manière cumulative. pressures_info: Mauvais réaménagements de carrières, disparition des sites de reproduction (Zones humides, fontaines, lavoirs), empoissonnement, ragondins, rénovation de murs de pierres, harassement de haies, fermeture du milieu, pollutions, artificialisation des sols, chytrides, changements climatiques, isolement des populations et fragmentation des habitats sont des pressions et menaces souvent identifiées (Sirugue et Varanguin, 2012, LPO, 2015, Grossi, 2012, Teynié, 2018, OAA, 2017...).

pressures_source: Despite the identification in the 2013 assessment of numerous low and medium-level threats and pressures, it is clear now that the population of this species has been increasing significantly and steadily. On this basis, and given the widespread distribution and very large population present in the country, no threats or pressures are considered significant at this point.

pressures_info:
Minke whales may be subject to a number of local and/or regional environmental pressures and threats throughout their North Atlantic range and in Irish coastal/offshore waters. In the development and preparation of the Conservation Plan for Cetaceans in Irish waters (DEHLG, 2009), a comprehensive review of the pressures and threats believed and/or documented to be acting on this species and/or its functional group was undertaken.
The main pressures thought to be acting on this species in Irish waters are considered to involve commercial shipping-based or vessel-based activities that occur primarily on a local or regional scale and/or on a temporary or intermittent basis (e.g., impacts arising from shipping movements or geophysical seismic exploration; impacts from local/regional prey removal by fisheries; DEHLG, 2009).
Since current minke whale distribution is known to be both offshore Atlantic and also inshore/coastal around Ireland, where a pressure may be regionally intensive the ranking given is one of medium importance.
It should be noted that in relation to seismic exploration, which tends to occur on a local or occasionally regional scale in the waters of Ireland’s EEZ and is comparatively low in coverage relative to several other member states, a robust regulatory and management regime applies in order to avoid potentially significant impacts on all species of marine mammal.
This regime takes cognisance of recent scientific research and surveillance findings (e.g., Baines et al., 2013; Berrow & O’Brien, 2013; Cronin & Barton, 2014; O’Brien & Berrow, 2014; Kavanagh et al., 2017; O’Donnell et al., 2017; O’Donnell et al., 2018a,b; O’Sullivan & Manning, 2017; Berrow et al., 2018; O’Brien & Berrow, 2018; Rogan et al., 2018) and it is expected that this practice will continue into the future.
There is no evidence to suggest a change in the main pressures thought to be acting on this species in the near future (i.e., threats). However surveillance of the species and the pressures potentially acting upon it will continue, while the application of strong management measures (e.g., via the statutory/regulatory process) to avoid potentially significant impacts is also expected to continue.

pressures_info: IAS union concern :  Asclepias syriaca L.;

Pressures and threats were documented as part of routine surveillance. Data were also available from the 2010 draft Sub-basin Plans for 27 catchments. Additional information came from reported incidents (including through site-inspection), monitoring of authorised activities (following AA/EIA) and KerryLIFE (LIFE13 NAT/IE/000144), as well as other NPWS projects and WFD and EPA reports. Pressures were ranked according to their importance in the eight priority pearl mussel catchments. A large number of pressures were identified and are itemised below. Only ten could be listed on the form, only five of which could be listed as of high importance.
Knowledge of the species’ ecological flow requirements has improved in recent years (e.g. Moorkens and Killeen, 2014; Moorkens et al., 2015). Changes in hydrological regime, particularly reduced base flow, but also increased scour at high flows, have been demonstrated to impact significantly on populations in Ireland, alone and in combination with pollution. Hydrological change in pearl mussel catchments is largely a result of land drainage (for agriculture (A31), forestry (B27) and peat extraction (C05)), but also results from abstraction for drinking water (F33) and, in at least one catchment (Clady), impoundment for electricity generation (D02).
F31 – Other modification of hydrological conditions for residential or recreational development – was used to cover the construction of fishery weirs in freshwater pearl mussel habitat in the Owenriff, which led to morphological changes in the habitat and severe impacts on flow. Owing to the international importance of this population, this pressure was assessed as of High importance.
Pollution with fine sediment and nutrients continues to impact on the species. Agriculture is a primary source, notably land reclamation and intensification (A01, A02, A04, A05, A13, A20, A31), increased stocking densities and housing of stock, leading to the generation of slurry (A19), and over-grazing (A09). Code A26 for diffuse pollution was used to cover all of these agricultural sources, although with drainage of peatland and wet soils, agricultural pollution can effectively be point source. Physical alteration of river banks, including bank reinforcement (A33) continues to be a significant pressure in pearl mussel rivers and is linked to intensification of agricultural use. A30, abstractions for agriculture, is also a concern, but it has not yet been demonstrated to be more than of low importance. As with other abstraction pressures, A30 may become a more significant threat, in combination with predicted droughts owing to climate change. Direct impacts have been recorded owing to livestock (cattle) in rivers, in at least six catchments, including three priority catchments. Some of the incidents have already been addressed through KerryLIFE (LIFE13 NAT/IE/000144) and it is hoped others in the eight priority catchments will be addressed through the Pearl Mussel Project European Innovation Partnership. Cattle in rivers have caused direct disturbance and death of mussels and habitat damage, as well as being a point-source of pollution. This pressure could have, therefore, been reported under a number of different pressure codes, e.g. A14 – Livestock farming (without grazing), K05 - Physical alteration of water bodies, but is considered to be covered, along with most other agricultural pressures, by A26.
As well as causing hydrological impacts, drainage (C05, B27, A31) and other disturbance of peatland (e.g. over-grazing by sheep (A09) or wind farm development (D01, D08)) leads to pollution, through mineralization of peat and increased losses of ammonia, dissolved and particulate organic fractions and mineral sub-soil. Furthermore, damage to wetland areas reduces the detrital food supply for adult and juvenile mussels. Organic pollutants, as well as increasing water colour and turbidity, cause enrichment through increased biomass of the bacteria and fungi. The loss of organic acids from drained and degraded peatland has also been demonstrated to result in acid episodes is Irish streams.
Conifer forest on peatland releases high pollutant loads (B23). Significant losses of pollutants occur following tree-felling (B09, B12), where slow decomposition of conifer needles, branches and roots is a significant source of organic matter and, ultimately, nutrients. Ground disturbance by machinery can also contribute to losses of inorganic sediment and particulate organic matter. Direct loss of dissolved nutrients can occur during application of synthetic fertilisers (B19), which is required to establish, and bring to maturity, conifer crops on peatland. 
Pollution with human waste-water (F12) is also an on-going concern, particularly from on-site systems at tourist attractions (e.g. in the Dawros and Bundorragha), but also from urban agglomerations (e.g. possible leakages from sewerage network in Owenriff). EPA (2018) highlighted that urban waste-water treatment needs to improve at 13 urban areas to protect the freshwater pearl mussel.
Maintenance of flood-relief/arterial drainage schemes is an on-going pressure, including in at least two SAC populations (F28). A number of pearl mussel populations are also threatened by new flood-relief schemes.
Operation and maintenance of bridges, roads and other transport infrastructure (E01 Roads, paths, railroads and related infrastructure (e.g. bridges, viaducts, tunnels)) continues to be a pressure in pearl mussel catchments. New and upgraded transport infrastructure is still a threat.
Although KerryLIFE (LIFE13 NAT/IE/000144) and the Pearl Mussel Project European Innovation Partnership are addressing agricultural pressures, these are voluntary schemes limited to priority catchments. As a result, all pressures are likely to continue and have also been listed as threats.
Other than the construction of fishery weirs in the Owenriff, there has been no genuine change in pressures or threats for this reporting cycle. Both the reporting form and Article 17 pressures and threats list have changed since Article 17 2013.

Reyne et al. (in prep.) provide a detailed and up-to-date assessment of the current pressures impacting on the natterjack toad in Kerry. Only pressures identified at more than 5% of sites are listed in 8.1. Pressures impacting more than 10% of sites are considered to be of High importance.
Poor water quality (often characterised by algal blooms) was the most common threat, followed by lack of grazing/grassland management and interspecific faunal relationships (i.e. predation of tadpoles and eggs by invertebrates). Natural succession (L02) relates to ponds becoming overgrown with emergent vegetation, making them unsuitable for breeding, while the invasive species recorded (I02) were Crassula helmsii and Hippophae rhamnoides.
All current pressures are also listed as threats as they are likely to continue into the future without concerted intervention.
Poor water quality was not noted amongst the main threats in the 2013 assessment, suggesting that this is a relatively new problem for these sites. It was noted at artificial ponds as well as natural sites and was particularly evident at Lough Yganavan. Water abstraction was included in 2013 and is mentioned as a potential concern in Reyne et al. (in prep.), but it is not clear to what extent this is a real pressure and so it not listed this time. Further information is needed on the pattern and impacts of water abstraction on the dune slacks in the Magharees in particular.
Although not included as a threat yet, as it may be expected to act over a longer time frame, the potential for climate change to impact on the conservation status of the toad merits further investigation, in particular given the demonstrated effect of seasonal rainfall and temperature patterns on the species’ reproductive success (Reyne et al., in prep.).
pressures_source: Reyne, M. et al. (in prep.) Natterjack toad (Epidalea calamita) monitoring and conservation status 2016-2018. Irish Wildlife Manuals 1XX. Department of Culture, Heritage and the Gaeltacht, Dublin, Ireland. www.npws.ie/publications

pressures_info:
The turtles that migrate through Irish waters mate and breed in the tropics. The main known pressures relate to anthropogenic mortalities of adults, eggs and juveniles at nesting beaches in the Caribbean and northern coast of South America (e.g. French Guiana). These include direct predation by humans and dogs, disturbance of nesting females, damage to nests and eggs, and mortality of hatchlings due to disorientation arising from artificial light pollution and other tourist-related activities. Entanglement in fishing equipment in the coastal waters adjacent to nesting beaches can also lead to mortality. All of these are grouped in 8.1 as “Threats and pressure from outside the EU” and although further quantification is needed, they are considered to be of High importance.
Long-line fishing in the Pacific is a significant cause of mortality, and this is also considered a risk in the North Atlantic (Lewison et al., 2004; Pierpoint, 2000; Huang, 2015), although the impact has not been fully quantified. Bycatch in fishing gear generally has also been identified as a significant concern (e.g. Hamelin et al., 2017). In Irish coastal waters animals have died and been injured as a result of becoming entangled in ropes associated with lobster and crab fisheries. Collisions with pleasure boats has been reported as a cause for concern for turtles in some waters, but this is not thought to be a significant issue in Irish waters.
Despite successes at some tropical beaches, e.g. in Trinidad where nesting beaches are carefully patrolled and tourist disturbance is restricted, the current pressures are considered likely to continue as threats into the foreseeable future. Further research is required on the extent and severity of the impact of these on the North Atlantic population of leatherback turtle.

A number of high-importance threats are listed including A19, A20, G12 and N03. These are also considered to be pressures of medium importance in the current reporting period. 
Enrichment and nutrient pollution from various sources (A19 and A20) can result in profusions of macrophytes and filamentous algae, obscuring gravel substrates in spawning areas and potentially diminishing their value as egg development habitats. Reduced water quality can impact on both larval and adult stages (Maitland & Hatton?Ellis, 2003; King & Roche, 2008; Maas & Breine, 2008). A number of important spawning estuaries for twaite shad were at moderate or poor ecological status in the 2010-2015 WFD assessment (https://www.catchments.ie/maps/) by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Some are at risk of not meeting surface water environmental objectives, with diffuse phosphorus loss to surface water identified as a major pressure. Twaite shad spawn at a time when water temperatures are on the rise. Higher water temperatures can lead to a growth in aquatic plants and extensive areas of filamentous algae have been observed during survey work at twaite shad spawning grounds.
Adult shad (both twaite and allis) are vulnerable to commercial fishing at sea and becoming recorded as bycatch (G12). The extent of this is not known but recorded levels of the twaite shad in landings are low. 
When aspects of life history such as adult migration, spawning, egg survival, hatching and juvenile maturation are dependent on specific flow patterns, any pronounced increase in discharge (N03) could result in decreased or complete failure of recruitment for that year (Humphries & Lake, 2000; Bunn & Arthington, 2002). For spawning twaite shad in Irish catchments, the timing and scale of summer flood events (Lennon & Walsh, 2008, Walsh, 2016) are of concern, particularly given the increased likelihood of such incidents in the future (Sweeney et al., 2008; Graham & Harrod, 2009; Fealy et al., 2018). Major flood events occurred during the twaite shad spawning periods in 2012 and 2017. It is likely that these events had a significant impact on recruitment in those years (Habitats Directive and Red Data Book Species, Executive Reports, 2012 & 2017).
Estuarine habitats serve as important nurseries for juvenile twaite shad as well as locations for adult congregation during spawning migrations. Estuaries are, however, highly vulnerable to habitat disturbance and environmental pressures, both natural and man-made (Vasconcelos et al., 2007), with the effects of pollution, dredging, shipping, channel and harbour maintenance (E03) posing immediate and long-term threats to resident and transient species, including twaite shad.
A large population of the Asian clam, Corbicula fluminea, has been recorded within kilometres of the twaite shad spawning ground on the River Barrow (I02). C. fluminea is considered to be of major risk to biodiversity in Ireland and could pose a threat to twaite shad (http://nonnativespecies.ie/wp-content/uploads/2014/03/Corbicula-fluminea-Asian-Clam1.pdf). This invasive species has the capacity to disperse and colonise extensively and to a dense level across the river bed, where fertilised shad eggs may drop down and settle for some days prior to hatching. It is not known if Asian clam is currently impacting on twaite shad in the R. Barrow but this ‘intragravel’ phase is vulnerable to substantial loss should the C. fluminea population expand over the shads’ spawning ground. 
Barriers to migration, particularly in the form of weirs, can impede or prevent twaite shad accessing spawning habitat in designated rivers in Ireland (D02). A further issue is the potential for hybridisation (Coscia et al., 2010) between converging populations of twaite and allis shad simultaneously obstructed below barriers. The R. Barrow (one of the SAC rivers for twaite shad) is a navigation river. A substantial weir at the upper tidal limit of this river, followed by a series of navigation weirs, prevent upstream migration of twaite shad and impact on the availability of spawning habitat in this river.
Other likely pressures and threats for A. fallax include marine fish harvesting (G01) leading to a reduction in prey populations at sea and mortality from recreational fishing (G06) during the freshwater spawning phase.

Changes in agricultural practices are the main pressure, in particular intensification of grassland usage (switches from extensive grazing/rough grazing to silage production). The High ranking relates to the fact that this pressure acts over large areas in Ireland. Significant mortality of leverets (and perhaps adults) has been associated with intensive mowing operations, i.e. silage harvesting, although the full extent of this is hard to quantify. Conversion of grasslands to forestry reduces habitat quality for hares. Road kill remains a concern. Disturbance and mortality due to illegal hunting (e.g. use of lurchers) is also a localised problem although the full impact on the population is not known. Although licensed coursing leads to some mortality each year, management of coursing reserves has also been shown to be beneficial to hares leading to higher local hare densities (Reid et al., 2010). All the identified pressures are also included as potential threats given that the issues are ongoing. In addition the risk associated with the expansion from Northern Ireland of the brown hare population (i.e. genetic introgression) is also included as a threat (Caravaggi et al., 2016).

The species is found in one lake habitat only and has never been found outside it. Any pressure or threat that posed an extinction problem would be catastrophic for the species. 
In the most recent WFD assessment (2010-2015), L. Leane had a good ecological status (https://www.catchments.ie/maps/) and is not at risk of failing to meet surface water environmental objectives. The lake has been subject to pressures over the past 40 years and the Killarney shad has survived. Advanced nutrient treatment at the Killarney WWTP contributes significantly to moderating nutrient input from a major point source. While L. Leane is currently at good ecological status, pollution from urban waste water (F12) is considered a medium threat over the next 12 years. Enrichment and nutrient pollution from both point and diffuse sources in the catchment of L. Leane could result in profusions of macrophytes and filamentous algae, obscuring gravel substrates in spawning areas and potentially diminishing their value as egg development habitats. Reduced water quality can impact on both larval and adult stages (Maitland & Hatton?Ellis, 2003; King & Roche, 2008; Maas et al., 2008).  Any enrichment could contribute to reduced oxygen levels in the lake’s hypolimnion during periods of thermocline formation. The hypolimnion, or lower water layers in the lake, below the epilimnion, is important in providing cooler water for this pelagic species.
The present fish fauna in Lough Leane is notable for the absence of pike, a top predator, and of rapidly-colonising fish species such as roach or dace. Any introduction of non-native or non-resident fish species (G08) could have detrimental effects on the Killarney shad, through predation from pike and via competition for zooplankton from roach and dace (L06). However, land-locked shad species in Greek lakes have been shown to predate on 0-group roach in shallow littoral areas (Bobori et al., 2001). 
The introduction of the invasive zebra mussel or Asian clam (I02) to L. Leane could impact on the resident Killarney shad population. While National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS) implement strict regulatory controls on watercraft using the lake, this is considered to be a medium-level threat for the foreseeable future.

Humpback whales may be subject to a number of local and/or regional environmental pressures and threats throughout their North Atlantic range and in Irish offshore waters. In the development and preparation of the Conservation Plan for Cetaceans in Irish waters (DEHLG, 2009), a comprehensive review of the pressures and threats believed and/or documented to be acting on this species and/or its functional group was undertaken.
The main pressures thought to be acting on this species in Irish waters are considered to involve commercial shipping-based or vessel-based activities that occur primarily on a local or regional scale and/or on a temporary or intermittent basis (e.g., impacts arising from shipping movements or geophysical seismic exploration; impacts from local/regional prey removal by fisheries; DEHLG, 2009).
Since current humpback whale distribution is shown to be offshore Atlantic but also inshore/coastal in nature, where a pressure may be regionally intensive, the ranking given is one of medium importance due also to the species’ depleted population status.
It should be noted that in relation to seismic exploration, which tends to occur on a local or occasionally regional scale in the waters of Ireland's EEZ and is comparatively low in coverage relative to several other member states, a robust regulatory and management regime applies in order to avoid potentially significant impacts on all species of marine mammal.
This regime takes cognisance of recent scientific research and surveillance findings (e.g., Ryan, 2012; Ryan et al., 2015; Kavanagh et al., 2017; Berrow et al., 2018; Rogan et al., 2018) and it is expected that this practice will continue into the future.
There is no evidence to suggest a change in the main pressures thought to be acting on this species in the near future (i.e., threats). However surveillance of the species and the pressures potentially acting upon it will continue, while the application of strong management measures (e.g., via the statutory/regulatory process) to avoid potentially significant impacts is also expected to continue.
