pressures_info:
Bottlenose dolphins may be subject to a number of local and/or regional environmental pressures and threats throughout their North Atlantic range (Hammond et al., 2012) and in Irish coastal/offshore waters. In the development and preparation of the Conservation Plan for Cetaceans in Irish waters (DEHLG, 2009), a comprehensive review of the pressures and threats believed and/or documented to be acting on this species and/or its functional group was undertaken.
The main pressures thought to be acting on this species in Irish waters are considered to involve commercial shipping-based or vessel-based activities that occur primarily on a local or regional scale and/or on a temporary or intermittent basis (e.g., impacts arising from geophysical seismic exploration; impacts from local/regional prey removal by fisheries or by-catch in fisheries; DEHLG, 2009).
Since the species is known to be broadly pelagic and oceanic in its ecology in the North-East Atlantic (Wells & Scott, 2009; Hammond et al., 2012) and current Bottlenose dolphin distribution is very wide in its spatial and temporal occurrence, where a pressure may be regionally intensive the ranking given is one of medium importance.
It should be noted that in relation to seismic exploration, which tends to occur on a local or occasionally regional scale in the waters of Ireland's EEZ and is comparatively low in coverage relative to several other member states, a robust regulatory and management regime applies in order to avoid potentially significant impacts on all species of marine mammal.
This regime takes cognisance of recent scientific research and surveillance findings (e.g., Baines et al., 2013; Hammond et al., 2017; Kavanagh et al., 2017; Berrow et al., 2018; Rogan et al., 2018a; Rogan et al., 2018b) and it is expected that this practice will continue into the future.
There is no evidence to suggest a change in the main pressures thought to be acting on this species in the near future (i.e., threats). However surveillance of the species and the pressures potentially acting upon it will continue, while the application of strong management measures (e.g., via the statutory/regulatory process) to avoid potentially significant impacts is also expected to continue.

pressures_info:
Sperm whales may be subject to local and/or regional environmental pressures and threats throughout their North Atlantic range and in Irish inshore/offshore waters. In the development and preparation of the Conservation Plan for Cetaceans in Irish waters (DEHLG, 2009), a comprehensive review of the pressures and threats believed and/or documented to be acting on this species and/or its functional group was undertaken.
The main pressures thought to be acting on this species in Irish waters are considered to involve commercial shipping-based or vessel-based activities that occur primarily on a local or regional scale and/or on a temporary or intermittent basis (e.g., impacts arising from shipping movements or geophysical seismic exploration; DEHLG, 2009).
Since sperm whale distribution is known to be primarily offshore in the deeper Atlantic Ocean, where there is a documented pressure that may be regionally intensive the ranking given is one of medium importance, given also the species’ continued Vulnerable status (Taylor et al., 2008).
It should be noted that in relation to seismic exploration, which tends to occur on a local or occasionally regional scale in the waters of Ireland’s EEZ and is comparatively low in coverage relative to several other member states, a robust regulatory and management regime applies in order to avoid potentially significant impacts on all species of marine mammal.
This regime takes cognisance of recent scientific research and surveillance findings (e.g., Baines et al., 2013; Hammond et al., 2017; Kavanagh et al., 2017; Rogan et al., 2017; O’Donnell et al., 2018; Berrow et al., 2018; Rogan et al., 2018) and it is expected that this practice will continue into the future.
There is no evidence to suggest a change in the main pressures thought to be acting on this species in the near future (i.e., threats). However surveillance of the species and the pressures potentially acting upon it will continue, while the application of strong management measures (e.g., via the statutory/regulatory process) to avoid potentially significant impacts is also expected to continue.

pressures_info: De Nauwe korfslak is sterk afgenomen door de volgende factoren: afplaggen van duingebieden en verwijderen van geschikte vegetaties als duindoorns met als vervolgbeheer begrazing ikv herstel Grijze duinen. Daarnaast is de soort afgenomen door verwijderen van populierachtige boomsoorten in het kader van exotenbeleid. Deze boomsoorten, die lang niet allemaal als exoot hoeven te worden beschouwd, zorgen voor kalkrijk strooisel waar de Nauwe korfslak van afhankelijk is, zelfs in een omgeving die kalkarm is. Verder is Nauwe korfslak afgenomen door natuurlijke successie: opkomend eikenbos maar ook sterke verdichting van struwelen. Ook grondwaterstandveranderingen hebben plaatselijk gezorgd voor afname van populaties.

pressures_info:
Les principaux facteurs de menaces et de pression sont liés à l'eau. Les drainages anciens créés pour l'exploitation de la tourbe constituent le premier facteur de dégradation qui accentue l'évolution naturelle de l'habitat. Leur effet se cumule également à ceux du changement climatique : un manque de précipitation flagrant est remarqué depuis 2015 eu égard à l'augmentation des températures estivales donc de l'augmentation de l'évaporation et de l'évapotranspiration. Le programme LIFE de restauration des tourbières du Jura et une étude Natura 2000 doivent contribuer à mieux comprendre et restaurer le fonctionnement hydrologique de la station de Bannans. Des travaux de comblement de fossé de drainage devraient être entrepris en 2019.
Sur les sites récents (dont Bannans prioritairement), des réflexions doivent être entreprises sur l’évolution des pratiques sur les bassins versants (installations agricoles, maîtrise des épandages, besoins d’alimentation en eau potable en période touristique ou de sécheresse...) et son adéquation avec le maintien de habitat et de l’espèce :
- Pour Bannans, l’objectif de protection n’est pas réalisé : l’installation récente d’un GAEC au pourtour du site a été autorisée, au contact des zones humides. Il serait nécessaire de conduire une étude d’épandage et optimiser les épandages réalisés autour de l’installation. Une étude récente relative au réseau d’assainissement de Chaffois semble montrer la subsitance de pics de pollution qui ne devraient plus exister. Des mesures d’étude et de remédiation doivent être réalisées. 
- Pour Frasne, l’objectif de protection n’est pas réalisé à l’échelle du bassin versant : la restauration du fonctionnement hydrologique est pourtant la priorité pour restaurer le marais de l’Ecouland. En effet, le développement de l'urbanisme à vocation résidentielle et industriel et commercial à Frasne au pourtour de la Réserve naturelle constitue un fait passé d'altération de l'habitat de l'espèce : réduction de l'alimentation en eau du marais par le bassin versant urbanisé, utilisation du marais comme récolteur des eaux pluviales, réseau d'assainissement non finalisé tel que le prévoyait le plan d'assainissement de la commune et non entretien du réseau jusqu'au moment où la pollution fut irréversible.
- Pour les Pontets, le prélèvement AEP n'est toujours pas encadré par une règlementation précisant le niveau de prélèvement.

Les menaces possibles sont sans doute très variées et ponctuelles, chacune ayant probablement un effet limité sur les populations locales de grandes cigales, si tant est qu'on puisse parler de "population" car les individus sont assez dispersés.

pressures_info: Los cambios de uso agrícola, el empleo de herbicidas en campos y bordes de caminos o el pisoteo y la artificialización se cuentan entre las presiones más generalizables. La cercanía de parques eólicos o de repoblaciones en las inmediaciones de algunos núcleos pueden ser fuente de amenazadas futuras más intensas. Puede además verse perjudicada por la competencia natural con otras especies, como su congénere Sisymbrium runcinatum.

Los desbroces, que pueden llegar a favorecer a la especie, pueden también resultar perniciosos si son tan frecuentes o drásticos que impiden la recuperación. El consecuente desequilibrio en la estructura demográfica y la magnitud de las fluctuaciones que experimenta, supuestamente, el número de efectivos de las poblaciones, unido al carácter dioico de la especie, hace que estas poblaciones no puedan considerarse en buen estado de conservación, ni tampoco que se encuentren libres de amenaza de extinción. El manejo sostenible de las poblaciones puede conseguirse fácilmente mediante un adecuado tratamiento forestal de la zona, que conlleve la limpieza parcial y sucesiva, nunca simultánea, de los cortafuegos y de las pistas de esas zonas. Esta práctica viene siendo habitual en los últimos años por los responsables de la gestión forestal de la comarca del Rebollar (Villasrubias-Dehesa de El Jaque y El Payo), por lo cual bastaría con mantenerla.

La introducción de especies exóticas en su área de ocupación como son son el pez sol (Lepomis gibbosus) , el pez gato (Ameiurus melas), perca americana  (Micropterus salmoides), lucio (Esox lucius), lucioperca (Stizostedion lucioperca) y diversas especies de ciprínidos es una de las principales causas del declive de esta especie.

pressures_info: Other IAS: carpa (Cyprinus carpio), perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), pez sol (Lepomis gibbosus), alburno (Alburnus alburnus), gobio (Gobio lozanoi), lucio (Esox lucius).

Other IAS: Esta especie está amenazada por especies de moluscos exóticos e invasores como el mejillón cebra (Dreissena polymorpha) o la almeja asiática (Corbicula fluminea).
En toda su área de distribución, la sequía presenta una amenaza creciente capaz de eliminar rápidamente las poblaciones, que en verano quedan aisladas en pozas. La cuenca del Guadiana está invadida por la almeja asiática y enormemente regulada por presas y azudes. Además, existen gran número de embalses que no constituyen un hábitat idóneo para la especie, pero hacen de barrera para el movimiento de los peces hospedadores y la conexión entre poblaciones de náyades. Otra amenaza destacable se refiere a la eutrofización de la cuenca, por contaminación procedente de la agricultura y urbanismo (Araujo, 2012).
pressures_source: Se han estudiado los efectos de la almeja asiática (Corbicula fluminea) sobre las poblaciones de náyades autóctonos ibéricos, siendo patentes sus efectos negativos al configurar el hábitat y desplazar a estas especies (http://planhidrologico2015.chguadiana.es/corps/chguadiana/data/resources/file/redes_control/informes_publicaciones/informe_almeja_asiatica_anhidra.pdf).

pressures_source: Criterio experto

Tal y como indican Guallart y Templado (2012), la especie posiblemente no requiere de medidas especiales de conservación, por no sufrir impactos humanos directos de importancia aparentes. 
A pesar de ello resultaría necesario conocer con precisión la ubicación de las principales poblaciones y controlar las actividades humanas que pudieran llevarse a cabo en las zonas donde se encuentran enclavadas.
Entre ellas, la ubicación de emisarios submarinos, tendidos submarinos, etc., debería realizarse teniendo en cuenta la localización de las poblaciones de este erizo. 
Asimismo, debería prohibirse o limitarse la pesca de arrastre en las zonas donde se hayan detectado poblaciones de la especie.

drástica en las capturas accidentales de tortuga boba. En relación con otras artes y aparejos de pesca  como son los artes de arrastre de fondo y artes menores que inciden en la captura de tortugas marinas en Cataluña, Valencia y Andalucía, diversas publicaciones de autores españoles son de interés para mejorar la gestión de las tortugas marinas es la región Mediterránea (Tomás et al., 2008; Álvarez de Quevedo et al., 2009; Cardona et al., 2009; Marco et al., 2015; Clusa et al., 2016 y Domenech et al., 2015).
Una amenaza a la que se debe prestar especial atención es la embolia gaseosa (García-Párraga et al., 2014; Fahlman et al., 2017) producida principalmente por la captura de tortugas en redes de arrastre de fondo. La liberación de los ejemplares vivos y aparentemente en buen estado puede llevar asociada por el contrario una mortalidad que puede ser alta, lo que incrementaría notablemente la mortalidad asociada a la captura incidental con artes de pesca en el Mediterráneo. 
Otras amenazas para la especie son el tráfico marítimo (código E02), variaciones en el nivel del mar debido al cambio climático (código N04), y otras presiones existentes fuera del territorio europeo (código Xe). Entre las amenazas emergentes para las tortugas, los plásticos han comenzado a ser estudiados (Domenech et al 2019)  en aguas españolas del mar Mediterráneo español.

IAS union concern :  Asclepias syriaca L.;

Ireland’s River Basin Management Plan (RBMP) 2018-2021 (https://www.housing.gov.ie/sites/default/files/publications/files/rbmp_report_english_web_version_final_0.pdf) identifies the significant pressures impacting on water bodies and freshwater communities at a national level and identifies corresponding actions that will be taken to address these.  The key pressures considered to pose threats / challenges to Atlantic salmon include:
1.  	Water quality from:
a.  	agricultural production, domestic waste-water treatment systems and forestry which are key sources of both rural diffuse and point-source pollution; and
b.  	urban waste-water pressures which are a source of point-source pollution.
2.  	Hydromorphological threats relating to physical modification or damage to habitat and natural river/lake processes, and changes functions caused by channelisation, drainage, dams, weirs, barriers and locks, overgrazing, embankments and culverts.
3.  	Climate change which has been identified by IFI as one of the greatest threats facing fish populations including salmon in the medium- to long-term. Temperature changes may shift the phenologies of anadromous fishes. The consequences of such changes are unknown, but the change has the potential to disrupt established ecological relationships at various life history stages.
4.  	Future warming may intensify the severity of floods and droughts, lessening the frequency of successful annual reproduction for anadromous fishes.
5.  	Certain invasive alien species have the potential to negatively impact wild salmon habitat in the freshwater or estuarine environment in Ireland through both direct and indirect means, although documented knowledge is limited. For example, the Asian clam (which has a limited distribution in Ireland to date) or certain invasive plant species have some potential to directly clog salmon spawning areas where present in abundance. The bank burrowing activities of Chinese mitten crab (confined to a single catchment but may now be extirpated there) and non-native crayfish (not recorded in rivers in Ireland to date) could increase sedimentation rates in impacted rivers and reduce the availability of suitable spawning habitat for native wild salmon. Invasive riparian plant species such as Himalayan balsam and knotweeds may also have such impacts by reducing bank stability during their winter die-back and concurrently increase sediment inputs.  If Pacific pink salmon increase their presence in Irish waters in future, an abundance of their decaying carcasses after spawning in a river system may have potential impacts on ecosystem functioning which could indirectly induce habitat modification as a consequence of increased nutrient input. 

The NASCO Implementation Plan for the period 2019-2024 for Atlantic salmon in Ireland (which is currently in a consultation process before finalisation in summer 2019) also highlights these key threats in addition to the following:
1.	Illegal catches, both at sea and within rivers which remain a concern and an impediment to stock recovery; 
2.	Over-reporting of catches which can affect population estimates and negatively impact stock assessments and their sustainable management; and
3.	Impacts of salmon aquaculture on native wild stocks from disease and escapes.
The current NASCO implementation plan in operation also identifies the above threats and pressures (NASCO (2013). CNL(13)44 NASCO Implementation Plan for the period 2013-18 EU-Ireland http://www.nasco.int/pdf/2013%20papers/CNL(13)44FINAL.pdf).

pressures_info: Pressures impacting on whiskered bats can be divided into those affecting roosts and those reducing the quality of their foraging habitat. The former include the renovation/demolition/disturbance of buildings used as summer roosts. The repair of road bridges over rivers is also a potential concern, as this species will roost in the crevices of masonry bridges. Mixed woodlands provide important foraging habitat for these bats; unsympathetic forest management practises can have negative impacts. Although these pressures are noted here for completeness, none of them is considered to be having a significant impact on the whiskered bat population in Ireland. This is due in part to an effective system of legal protection and in particular a widespread understanding among local authorities of the licensing requirements in relation to bat roost disturbance.

A number of pressures were listed in 2013: Roadkill data from 2007-2012 (from biology.ie) reported 10-30 otters killed on Irish roads each year. Roadkill was considered a Medium pressure in 2013 and while otters are still killed on Irish roads, this is not considered to pose a risk to conservation status nationally. The network of mammal underpasses, on new roads in particular, and the erection of road signs at known locations of repeated RTAs are examples of positive measures that have been taken to reduce otter roadkill. 
Entanglement in fixed fishing nets (e.g. tangle nets) and pots (e.g. fyke nets / lobster pots) was also a concern although it is difficult to estimate the level of resulting mortality due to limited reporting (Poole et al., 2007). Otter guards are now used on fyke nets by IFI during their surveys, reducing the risk from this threat. A monitoring study by the Marine Institute of by-catch in fishing gear is also underway. 
Diffuse and point-source pollution of freshwaters and coastal waters is likely to impact otters indirectly through changes to prey abundance. 
These threats are considered to produce local impacts only and are not listed above in 8.1.

Par le passé, les atteintes les plus fréquentes constatées au niveau des stations de Sphaigne de La Pylaie ont été les mêmes que celles qui concernent classiquement de nombreuses landes humides et tourbeuses : le défrichement agricole (destruction de la lande pour installer une prairie ou culture) accompagné ou non de drainage. Des cas avérés de destruction de stations de Sphagnum pylaesii se sont ainsi produits dans les deux dernières décennies sur Botsorhel (sur la marge du site de Guernelohet) et sur Ploudiry (Cleuz Dreïn - Ty Ménez) ; les plantations de résineux, souvent accompagnées de drainage, en forêts privées, mais parfois aussi communales (Argol), dans tous les grands secteurs où la Sphaigne de La Pylaie est ou était présente : monts d’Arrée, complexe du Ménez-Hom et de manière importante dans les Montagnes noires ; la création de plans d’eau à usage de chasse a pu être aussi dans le passé un facteur de destruction ; les carrières, exploitations de gisements notamment de kaolin. Un cas récent a donné lieu à une transplantation de stations menacées en Loqueffret. D’autres atteintes ou menaces ont pu être localement constatées, comme le dépôt de rémanents (arbustes et branchages coupés sur talus) sous les lignes électriques THT et laissés en place, comblant partiellement une mare à Sphagnum pylaesii (Ouest de Kerflaconnier en Brennilis, obs. J. Durfort, 2018).
De par les statuts de protection dont bénéficient désormais un nombre important de sites abritant l’espèce, ce type d’atteintes ou menaces est actuellement moins prégnant, notamment dans les monts d’Arrée. Cependant, elles peuvent encore impacter les sites ne bénéficiant pas des statuts de protection suffisants ou de grands sites protégés (Natura 2000) dans lesquels la veille ne peut pas toujours couvrir l’ensemble du territoire. 
Sur les sites désormais protégés par leur statut foncier, c’est aujourd’hui généralement l’abandon des pratiques agricoles traditionnelles qui constitue la principale menace (disparition des stations par fermeture lente du couvert végétal, concurrence par Campylopus introflexus). En effet, si certaines landes tourbeuses ou tourbières peuvent se révéler très stables dans le temps et abriter durablement des habitats favorables au maintien de la Sphaigne de La Pylaie, d’autres stations, présentes de manière disséminées dans des habitats tributaires d’une gestion favorable (fauche avec exportation principalement) peuvent se trouver fragilisées par l’abandon de la gestion.

pressures_info: Le climat est une pression et menace souvent identifiée chez cette espèce se reproduisant dans des zones à faibles profondeur d'eau, souvent temporaires, et donc très dépendantes des précipitations (Barrioz, 2015, CDPNE, 2017). Érosion marine, pollution, urbanisation, reboisements, mortalité routière, isolation et dégradations diverses des zones humides sont les pressions et menaces les plus communes (Barrioz, 2015, CDPNNE, 2017, Berroneau, 2014).

pressures_info:
Commentaires strictement identiques à 2006 et 2012 : Les réelles causes du déclin des populations enregistré dès la ?n du 19ème siècle et au cours de la première moitié du 20ème sont méconnues. Néanmoins, on s’accorde sur les principales causes suivantes : la chasse très prisée dès le XIXème au
point d’attirer des chasseurs étrangers (dé?nitivement interdite qu’à partir de 1956 (Dubray, 1985), après une première longue interdiction totale, mais apparemment peu respectée, entre 1927 et 1949 – cette chasse avait même déjà été interdite en 1913 et 1914), le braconnage, les grands incendies,
et la compétition possible avec la très forte activité pastorale à cette époque. Les principales pressions actuelles sont les pressions sur l’habitat, les dérangements liés au développement accéléré des activités de sports et de loisirs de nature (randonnées, raquettes, canyoning, véhicules motorisés,…), et toujours le braconnage sur certains secteurs malgré le renforcement très net de la police de la chasse. Si la chasse au Mou?on est strictement interdite, les battues au Sanglier limitent très certainement les possibilités de colonisation des populations. Les pressions sur l'habitat sont devenues très fortes et semblent s’accélérer : les grands incendies, certains aménagements qui augmentent les sources de dérangements et de braconnage (amélioration et extension du réseau routier, création de nouvelles pistes ouvertes au public ou de structures touristiques), et la trop grande fermeture des couverts forestiers suite à la déprise pastorale qui réduit le potentiel trophique des milieux. Les menaces futures sont quasiment identiques. Maudet et Dubray (2002) ayant démontré l’existence de nettes différences génétiques entre les populations nord et sud qui se traduisent par de claires di?érences morphologiques (cornages des mâles et des femelles), il existe un risque réel de pollution génétique future d’une souche par l’autre en cas de mauvaise sélection géographique des futurs sites des réintroductions ; seul le maintien d'une nette séparation géographique entre ces deux souches permettra de préserver cette diversité génétique original. Par contre, des menaces potentielles (pollution génétique et contamination sanitaire par les troupeaux domestiques) ne semblent pas affecter actuellement ces populations.

pressures_info: In the last assessment the pressures and threats were: agricultural intensification; forest planting on open ground (native trees); artificial planting on open ground (non-native trees); forest replanting (native trees); forest replanting (non-native trees); forestry clearance and invasive non-native species. Understanding of the species in conifer plantations have shown that the species can occur in high population densities (in excess of what is in unplanted areas) and so afforestation is not considered a pressure or threat (see Reich et al., 2012, 2017; Johnson et al., 2017). The issues of agricultural intensification where it removes or alters the outcropping rocks in heaths and invasive non-native species (Rhododendron in woodland) do remain but are considered no more than medium importance. Burning has been an issue in areas where the species has been introduced in conifer forests and this is included as it poses a threat to all conifer plantation populations.

It is not clear what pressures or threats may impact on this species in Ireland. Elsewhere in its European range, mortalities at wind farms are a significant concern, particularly during the periods of migration (Rodrigues et al., 2015). It is not clear how, or if, the Nathusius’ pipistrelles found in Ireland migrate and the risk of collision with off-shore wind-farms in particular (e.g. in the Irish Sea) is limited. Nonetheless, this is a threat that merits monitoring, especially as plans for additional off-shore wind-farms are being prepared.
Nathusius’ pipistrelle still appears to be in a colonisation phase in Ireland. A breeding population is established in Northern Ireland but it is quite localised and has yet to spread significantly from its base around Lough Neagh. Although this species has been turning up consistently in the north-east of the Republic of Ireland, the records of this species in the rest of the country remain sporadic and disjunct. Several low and medium-level threats and pressures were listed in the 2013 assessment, but it seems unlikely that any of them are limiting its spread or establishment here. Lundy et al. (2010) predicted that climate change would underpin the further spread and establishment of this species and that may be the main determining factor in it reaching a favourable status in Ireland in the coming decades.
For now, no pressures are considered significant and only wind farms are listed as a potential threat.

Pressures and threats were ranked from data gathered during the 2016-2018 survey at 22 of the known 30 localities of P. ralfsii and further information on the occurrence and frequency of pressures at these localities can be found in Campbell et al. (in prep.). Details of pressures and threats were noted at the eight remaining localities during the NPWS Rare and Threatened bryophyte surveys (see Campbell et al., 2015).
No pressures were identified as being of high or medium importance nationally. Any high or medium-impact pressures recorded during surveys usually affected only small areas of the habitat for the species, or occurred at a low frequency across the localities surveyed.
Pressures that affected relatively large areas of the localities where they occurred, but which occurred at a low frequency across the localities surveyed, include A09 Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock and A10 Extensive grazing or undergrazing by livestock. In most cases where intensive and non-intensive grazing (mainly by sheep, cattle and rabbits) was recorded, it was regarded as either beneficial or not damaging to the habitat for the species. The negative pressure of undergrazing is thought to be implicated in the reason for the absence of P. ralfsii at three localities in Co. Kerry, but there is the potential for the species to return to these localities and undergrazing is not a pressure at a national scale. These sites are grazed by cattle, but changing the management to sheep grazing would result in a shorter, more open sward and less poaching and eutrophication. The issue of grazing is complex and a balance needs to be maintained between the beneficial effects (e.g. creating bare patches for pioneering P. ralfsii to colonise and keeping vegetation generally low and open) and the negative effects, such as poaching, erosion and eutrophication. Grazing is discussed in more detail in Campbell et al. (in prep.).
Sports, tourism and leisure activities (F07) such as walking, jogging, horseriding and non-motorised and motorised vehicle use was noted as an impact on the habitat for the species at many P. ralfsii localities, but this is beneficial, as trampling compresses the ground and keeps vegetation low, maintaining suitable conditions for P. ralfsii and also can create new niches for P. ralfsii by creating bare areas of substrate for subsequent colonisation. However, too much disturbance from these activities on a continuous basis can break up the substrate crust continually, making it harder for P. ralfsii to establish and maintain itself. This was noted as a high impact but only affecting a very small proportion of the habitat for the species at some localities and is not impacting at a national level. 
Coastal erosion (L01 Abiotic natural processes (e.g. erosion, silting up, drying out, submersion, salinization) was recorded as a negative pressure at a couple of localities but only affected small areas of the overall habitat for the species and was not listed as a pressure nationally. Other pressures that were high-impact but which affected only a small area of the habitat at a local level include L02 Natural succession resulting in species composition change (other than by direct changes of agricultural or forestry practices).
Climate change has not been added as an impact, but it is likely to affect the distribution of P. ralfsii in Ireland in decades to come if average temperatures continue to rise.
pressures_source: Pressures and threats were recorded during 2015-2018 field surveys at 22 of the known 30 localities of P. ralfsii in the Republic of Ireland (Campbell et al., in prep.). Details of pressures and threats were noted at the eight remaining localities during the NPWS Rare and Threatened Bryophyte surveys (see Campbell et al., 2015).

Crayfish Plague was confirmed in Ireland in 2015 by DNA testing which is the first case since the 1980s. This disease poses the most significant threat to the species as the evidence from elsewhere in Europe is that it causes 100% mortality of stocks of the species. As the disease was limited in occurrence to six well-separated locations, the pressure has been classified as medium importance. However the threat is to the entire national stock so is of high importance. Most of the disease outbreaks have been independent of each other (involving different strains of Aphanomyces astaci) but it cannot be ascertained how they arrived at the outbreak sites. Human-mediated transmission appears the most likely means of spread but there is no conclusive evidence. 
The threat from invasive non-indigenous crayfish species (NICS) remains high. At present there is no evidence that any NICS are present in Ireland. However, should they become established it would make control of the disease extremely difficult if not impossible. The importation to Ireland of the five crayfish species on the EU list of invasive alien species of Union concern has been prohibited by SI 354/2018, the European Union (Invasive Alien Species) (Freshwater Crayfish) Regulations 2018.
Whilst habitat is considered sufficient for the species, allowing it to occupy its expected range, there are some indications of impacts of watercourse management that need investigation. The unexplained die-offs such as that on Lough Owel in 2018 also need investigation.

Impacts at roosts and to foraging and commuting habitats have been identified as most important for this species. Alterations at buildings used by bats, including renovations, disturbance and lighting, as well as natural flooding at caves, are all known to have caused population declines and in some cases roost abandonment. Predation by cats and pine martens in roosts can also pose a threat. Alterations to commuting routes (e.g. hedgerow clearances) and the felling of foraging habitats can also have significant local impacts. The impact of anti-parasitic drugs on dung fauna, an important component of this bat’s diet in winter, is also a concern. These threats were identified in the last report and are also expected to continue into the future.

Light pollution has been identified as a particular concern for Myotis bats such as Daubenton’s bat (Matthews et al., 2015; Voigt et al. 2018). F24 has been selected to represent this pressure, although lighting from industrial developments and roadway developments also contribute to the problem. Despite some growing awareness of light pollution this pressure is likely to continue into the future and it is also listed as a threat. 
Ranking of importance is based on expert opinion on likely impact of the pressure on the species.
Removal of riparian vegetation, bridge repairs and drainage works may also provide some cause for concern for this species and these issues merit further study. There is no evidence to date of an impact on Daubenton’s bat distribution due to these issues and hence they are not listed in 8.1.

Known pressures, such as ingestion of marine litter by marine mammals (Simmonds, 2012), impact from noise (related to cetaceans) and collisions with ships have not yet been assessed in detail, and require further studies within Maltese waters. Even though there is a lack of data on the impact of these pressures in Maltese waters, these are omnipresent threats to cetaceans throughout the Mediterranean, and therefore are being taken into consideration.

Furthermore, reference made to sea traffic under pressure E02 (Shipping lanes and ferry lanes transport operations) does not refer to shipping lanes but rather shipping routes

pressures_info:
Pressures and threats presented in 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium, low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact where data were available. Data gathered from the sources listed in section 7.2 are summarised in the backing document ‘NSUH17_AR1719_pressures_assessment_01a.xlsx’. For the current reporting period data on pressures were available for the Slieve Mish Mountains SAC (002185) NSUH site (Perrin et al., 2014a); no negative pressures were recorded for habitat 4060 from the Caha Mountains SAC (000093) (Perrin et al., 2014b). Data from shapefiles on locations of wildfires that occurred in spring 2017 (provided by NPWS), locations of newly planted forestry within this reporting period (FIPS data) and locations of windfarms (provided by SEAI) were also utilised. Additional data from Hodd (2015, 2016) and O’Neill et al. (in prep.) were examined, with relevant data extracted and analysed. As the data from the current reporting period only represent a small proportion of the data collected to inform NPWS (2013a), the pressures and threats information presented in NPWS (2013a) were also used to inform the current reporting period.
In the previous reporting period sheep grazing in the uplands, both undergrazing and overgrazing, was recorded under ‘A04.02.02 non-intensive sheep-grazing’. It was recorded as a high-importance pressure and threat for the 4060 habitat (NPWS, 2013a). Using the crosswalk referenced in the backing document ‘NSUH17_AR1719_pressures_assessment_01a.xlsx’ to convert impact codes from the old reporting scheme to the new, A04.02.02 crosswalks to A10 (Extensive grazing or undergrazing by livestock). However there was no evidence from the NSUH sites surveyed in the previous reporting period, or from this reporting period, that A10 (undergrazing) was a pressure.
A09 (Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock) can lead to erosion and damage to the vegetation of the 4060 habitat. It was stated in NPWS (2013a) that “sheep grazing is widespread within the sites surveyed by the NSUH and, where levels of grazing are high, is problematic within this habitat”, while Hodd (2015, 2016) recorded A09 twice within this reporting period. The NSUH did not record A09 at either the Slieve Mish Mountains SAC (002185) or the Caha Mountains SAC (000093) (Perrin et al., 2014a, 2014b). Based on evidence collected in the previous and current reporting periods and expert opinion, A09 was retained as high-importance pressure for this reporting period.
F07 (Sports, tourism and leisure activities) was recorded as a medium-importance pressure in the previous reporting period (NPWS, 2013a), and was retained as a medium-importance pressure for this reporting period. It refers to hill walking, which is often concentrated on the ridges and summits where this habitat is found. It was recorded four times during the current reporting period by O’Neill et al. (in prep.), impacting on 100% of the 4060 habitat in each case, once at medium intensity and the rest at low intensity.
D01 (Wind, wave and tidal power) was recorded as a low importance pressure in the previous reporting period. A review of Irish wind farm developments has indicated that 40.3% of wind farms are located within 2 km of 4060 habitat. As wind farm data were, in general, provided as a single point for each wind farm development, the review located wind farms within a 2 km buffer zone around 4060 habitat using the grid references provided by the SEAI (Connected22012018.xlsx) and the 4060 distribution polygon shapefile. Fifteen randomly selected wind farms located in upland areas were mapped in order to get an idea of the footprint of wind farms in upland areas. An average radius of 2 km was calculated based on these mapped areas. It should be noted that the majority of 4060 polygons within the 4060 distribution shapefile that were within 2 km of a wind farm were from the NPWS (2007) data which mapped areas above 350 m as 4060 habitat (97.9% of the polygons). It should be noted that, although these polygons were also utilised for area, distribution and range calculations for this reporting period, they overestimate the distribution of 4060 at a site level. For this reason, D01 is retained as a low-importance pressure for this reporting period. Areas represented by this NPWS (2007) dataset will need to be validated to get a more accurate estimate of the impact of D01 on 4060 habitat for future reporting periods.
Empirical critical loads of nutrient nitrogen have been recommended for a number of European habitats (Bobbink & Hettelingh, 2011). These recommended ranges were refined and extended using plant species diversity for habitat-specific positive indicators species. The influence of nitrogen deposition on plant species diversity was assessed using Threshold Indicator Taxa Analysis (TITAN; Baker & King, 2010) following Wilkins et al. (2016) and the Probability of Plant Species occurrence (PROPS-CLF; Slootweg et al., 2015). 
The spatial distribution of total nitrogen deposition was mapped on a 2.5 km x 2.5 km grid resolution using observations of atmospheric nitrogen during the last decade, a period with limited changes in national emissions of nitrogen (EPA, 2018). Total nitrogen deposition included dry ammonia deposition based on network of passive gaseous ammonia samplers and modelled dry deposition velocity (Cathcart et al., 2018), and observations of wet deposition for nitrate and ammonium in rainfall (Aherne et al., 2014). The observations were mapped using regression kriging which used maps of nitrogen emissions (URL: projects.au.dk/mapeire) and climate (URL: www.met.ie/climate/available-data/mera) as spatial predictors.
The proportion of each habitat that exceeded the lower critical load value of the recommended range was calculated. The importance of the impact of Nitrogen Deposition based on this proportion was determined as follows: Low-importance pressure/threat: 5-10%; Medium-importance pressure: 10-25%; High-importance pressure: >25%.
Alpine heath was assigned a Critical value of 5; 26% of the habitat area exceeded this value. Therefore the pressure A27 Agricultural activities generating air pollution was assigned a High importance, as approximately 75 to 80% of total nitrogen deposition can be attributed to agricultural sources nationally.
The low-importance pressures A11 (Burning for agriculture) and erosion, recorded under L01 (Abiotic natural processes (e.g. erosion, silting up, drying out, submersion, salinization)), were recorded as low-importance pressures in the previous reporting period and were retained as low-importance pressures for the current reporting period. It is estimated that up to 0.2% of the national 4060 habitat area (0.26 km2) was burnt in the spring of 2017 (using NPWS_Approx_Wildfire_Locations_Polygon_2017.shp in conjunction with the 4060 distribution polygon shapefile). As this pressure impacted on a relatively small reported area of the national resource of 4060 habitat, the ranking of low importance was retained. L01 was recorded at two locations within this reporting period, both as high intensity. Due to the low frequency of this pressure, L01 was retained as low importance. 
The low-importance pressures A06 (Abandonment of grassland management (e.g. cessation of grazing or of mowing)), I02 (Other invasive alien species (other than species of Union concern)), I04 (Problematic native species) and J03 (Mixed source air pollution, air-borne pollutants), recorded in NPWS (2013a) but not in this reporting period, were retained as low-importance pressures for the current reporting period.
B01 (Conversion to forest from other land uses, or afforestation (excluding drainage)), E01 (Roads, paths railroads and related infrastructure (e.g. bridges, viaducts, tunnels)) and M05 (Collapse of terrain, landslide), recorded in the current reporting period only, were assessed as low-importance pressures. Approximately 0.01% of the known national 4060 habitat area (0.02 km2) is either adjacent to or has some evidence of new forestry (i.e. planted within the current reporting period) within it (using PrivateForests2016.shp in conjunction with the 4060 distribution polygon shapefile). It is assessed as a low-importance pressure for this reporting period due to the relatively small area of known 4060 habitat impacted, but actual areas impacted are likely to be higher, particularly outside of designated sites. E01 and M05 were assessed as low-importance pressures due to the fact that they occurred just once each, both with a small footprint on the 4060 habitat.
The three high- and medium-importance pressures reported in section 7.1 are also considered to be threats to the 4060 habitat in the future. Climate change (under two separate codes, N01 and N02) was assessed as a low-importance threat to 4060 habitat in the previous reporting period (NPWS, 2013a), and has been upgraded to medium importance for the current reporting period. Habitat 4060 has been highlighted as particularly sensitive to climate change (Zaghi, 2008), with climate change predicted to impact on the occurrence of arctic-alpine plants in Ireland (EPA, 2017; Wyse Jackson, 2007). The results of a recent study, based on predictive modelling, on potential impacts of climate change on oceanic montane plant communities suggest that there will be a reduction in area of all true montane habitats in Ireland, as defined by their characteristic species (Hodd et al., 2014). This study, which largely focused on montane heath habitats, reports that many of the plant communities potentially impacted occur within a number of the habitats listed under Annex I of the EU Habitats Directive including significant elements of 4060. Arctic-montane species in particular are reported in this study to see areas of suitable climate shift northwards or contract to higher elevations, while internationally important bryophyte communities are also considered to be under threat. The study points to a possible need to review current distribution of protected areas in the light of potential impacts of climate change on species geographic distribution. Climate change (N) has been deemed a significant threat and has been upgraded to medium importance. It can also impact indirectly, for example, through increasing the likely frequency of more intense fires. It needs further focused studies to elucidate its likely effect and to devise appropriate conservation strategies in sufficient time to minimise impacts.

Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), and more knowledge and data.

Cuvier’s beaked whales may be subject to local and/or regional environmental pressures and threats throughout their North Atlantic range and in Irish inshore/offshore waters. In the development and preparation of the Conservation Plan for Cetaceans in Irish waters (DEHLG, 2009), a comprehensive review of the pressures and threats believed and/or documented to be acting on this species and/or its functional group was undertaken.
The main pressures thought to be acting on this species in Irish waters are considered to involve commercial shipping-based or vessel-based activities that occur primarily on a local or regional scale and/or on a temporary or intermittent basis (e.g., impacts arising from shipping movements or geophysical seismic exploration; DEHLG, 2009).
Among the potential pressures that may act on this species is the use of military sonars in the deeper ocean and adjacent continental margins which, while not employed by the Irish Naval Service, is known and documented to occur in the waters of Ireland’s EEZ (e.g., McCauley, 2015; Berrow et al., 2018). Such anthropogenic sound sources have been implicated previously in a number of beaked whale stranding events (DAHG, 2014) and questions remain with regard to an unusual series of strandings of this and other beaked whale species in western Ireland, the UK and adjacent states in August-September 2018 (DCHG, 2018 unpublished data; IWDG, 2018 unpublished data).
Since Cuvier’s beaked whale distribution is known to be primarily offshore in the deeper Atlantic Ocean, where there is a documented pressure that may be regionally intensive the ranking given is one of medium importance, given relatively low population estimates for the species (Rogan et al., 2018; see also 6.2 above) and also uncertainty with regard to the species’ movements and population/breeding structure (Taylor et al., 2008).
It should be noted that in relation to seismic exploration, which tends to occur on a local or occasionally regional scale in the waters of Ireland’s EEZ and is comparatively low in coverage relative to several other member states, a robust regulatory and management regime applies in order to avoid potentially significant impacts on all species of marine mammal.
This regime takes cognisance of recent scientific research and surveillance findings (e.g., Baines et al., 2013; McCauley, 2015; Frouin-Mouy et al., 2017; Hammond et al., 2017; Kavanagh et al., 2017; Rogan et al., 2017; O’Donnell et al., 2018; Berrow et al., 2018; Rogan et al., 2018) and it is expected that this practice will continue into the future.
There is no evidence to suggest a change in the main pressures thought to be acting on this species in the near future (i.e., threats). However surveillance of the species and the pressures potentially acting upon it will continue, while the application of strong management measures (e.g., via the statutory/regulatory process) to avoid potentially significant impacts is also expected to continue.

pressures_info: De Wijngaardslak is een soort die vermeld wordt op bijlage V van de Europese Habitatrichtlijn, omdat de soort in andere landen wordt verzameld voor menselijk consumptie. In Nederland zijn er geen aanwijzingen dat dit op grote schaal gebeurd. Verder spelen er voor zover nu bekend geen andere drukfactoren die de soort bedreigen. Wel moet gezegd dat meer onderzoek nodig is om gefundeerder uitspraken te kunnen doen over drukfactoren en bedreigingen voor de Wijngaardslak.

Diffuse and point source pollution (A19, A20, F11, F12) may be having localised impacts on populations of L. planeri. There has been a 3% reduction in river water quality in the national territory since 2015 (EPA, 2018) and the main sources of nutrient inputs are agriculture (slurry and chemical fertilisers) and sewage (waste water treatment plants). It is acknowledged that more effort will need to be made in tackling diffuse pollution in Irish rivers. 
Activities that lead to hydrological changes and canalisation of river channels (A31) will impact on habitat availability for L. planeri in certain locations. Mean density of larval Lampetra was <5/m2 in a number of catchments and, in certain cases, this is likely to be attributable to historical and on-going hydrological modifications in water bodies in the form of arterial drainage. 
All pressures and threats included here are ranked as having medium-level impacts on the long-term viability of L. planeri as impacts are generally localised.
pressures_source: EPA (2018) Water Quality in 2017: an indicators report. http://www.epa.ie/pubs/reports/water/waterqua/waterqualityin2017anindicatorsreport.html

pressures_info:
Pressures impacting on Natterer’s bats can be divided into those affecting roosts and those reducing the quality of their foraging habitat. The former include the renovation/demolition/disturbance of buildings used as summer roosts. The repair of road bridges over rivers is also a potential concern, as this species will roost in the crevices of masonry bridges. Mixed woodlands provide important foraging habitat for these bats; unsympathetic forest management practices can have negative impacts. 
Although these pressures are noted here for completeness, none of them is considered to be having a significant impact on the Natterer’s bat population in Ireland. This is due in part to an effective system of legal protection and in particular a widespread understanding among local authorities of the licensing requirements in relation to bat roost disturbance.

pressures_source:
There are no specific research to evaluate what kind of pressures and threats exist. From monitoring data it comes out, that main pressure and threat is habitat overgrowing (if the tree cover is too dense, the number of flowering individuals decrease). Land use change by direct destruction of habitat may be other factor.
During research impact from beavers is mentioned (due to beavers activities the hidrological changes occured), as well as the overgrazing in grasslands, electric line cleaning.

pressures_info: Threats: Een grote verandering in de komende periode op de Noordzee (migratiegebied, foerageergebied) is de sterke groei van het aantal offshore wind activiteiten. In de periode tot 2030 wordt minimaal 7GW aan off shore windmolens gerealiseerd, een toename van meer dan 6.1 GW ten opzichte van nu. Wat daarvan het (cumulatieve) effect is op zeehonden is nog onbekend.

pressures_info: Las principales presiones sobre el hábitat se han considerado el pastoreo intensivo y aumento en infraestructuras. Las ovejas pastan en verano, cuando la planta está en flor, pudiendo llegar a perder todas las inflorescencias, por lo que se puede dificultar aún más la renovación y expansión de las poblaciones, ya de por sí maduras y con porcentaje de juveniles muy bajo. Además el efecto mecánico por pisoteo del ganado produce desarraigo en ejemplares. Las instalaciones de las pistas de Esqui de Javalambre y el Observatorio Astrofísico de Javalambre (OAJ) han supuesto reducciones de los efectivos en las poblaciones del Cerro de Javalambre y en la de El Buitre-Los Verdinales, respectivamente. Las obras de la última ampliación de las pistas de esquí ha afectado a uno de los núcleos de la población Cerro de Javalambre, mientras que para comunicar Arcos de las Salinas con el OAJ se ha abierto una carretera que ha atravesado parte de la población El Buitre-Los Verdinales. A todo ello hay que añadir los arreglos de la pista que cruza los altos, cada vez más transitada.

Como ya se ha comentado, la situación de la especie parece preocupante en resumen por:
(1) haber visitado localidades sin confirmación de la especie, y en algunas de ellas no haber encontrado habitat adecuado,
(2) por tener citas muy antiguas nunca reconfirmadas,
(3) por sufrir presiones destructivas algunas localidades como 'San Martín del Pimpollar', 'Navalosa' (quemadas en verano de 2017) o que el principal núcleo de la especie, en 'el Pinar de Hoyocasero', que contiene un 97% de los individuos de la especie, haya estado a punto de quemarse en otro incendio en verano de 2017, igual que la población de Sanabria.
Así mismo, se podría pensar que ha desaparecido recientemente de algún núcleo (Ej. La localidad de 'Peñausende', entre el periodo 2015-2017 ya que su cita es reciente) por excesivo pisoteo del ganado, pero por precaución se ha asumido la presencia en todas ellas.

Other IAS: Las especies que se mencionan a continuación, son especies introducidas de manera ilegal que están generando graves problemas a la fauna piscícola autóctona, ya que la mayoría no tienen depredadores y además su tasa de reproducción es muy alta. Dichas especies son: carpa (Cyprinus carpio), lucio (Esox lucius), blackbass o perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), salmón del Danubio (Hucho hucho), gambusia (Gambusia holbrooki), perca-sol (Lepomis gibbosus), trucha arcoiris (Oncothynchus mykiss), pez gato negro (Ameiurus melas) y siluro (Silurus glanis).

pressures_info: The pressures listed are expected to be threats as well. The codes and titles of the pressures and threats agree with the report format (DG Environment 2017). The pressures identified can be divided into two major categories with respect to their underlying drivers and the changes they induce; 1) the decrease of area due to cuttings and forestry operations and 2) the alteration of structure and composition due to insufficient or absent natural disturbances and dynamics. A number of indicators are used in the evaluation of pressures (Berglund 2019).

Exact figures and good forecast on how much plastic that is deposited at different shores is hard to get, and there are in some areas a (but uneven) NGO work on clening the shores from plastics and other rubbish.

Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), and more knowledge and data.

featurename: Calcareous and calcshist screes of the montane to alpine levels (Thlaspietea rotundifolii)
pressures_source: The absence of reports/research indicating there are any pressures/threats to this habitat is taken as support for reporting 'no pressures'/'no threats'.

pressures_info:
A20. Eutrophication effects due to high loads of agricultural nutrients to coastal waters causes less light penetration and risk of overgrowth of filamentous algae, which effects the vegetation. Due to measures for decreasing the nutrient load from agriculture, water quality improvements has been observed but the conditions are still not sufficient. According to guidelines this pressure (A20) should be used also when the source (natural or artificial) is unknown. The assessment medium is set due to the pressure having moderate influence in the habitat. The threat is set to medium as the improvement of the measures is expected to be slow.
E02. The waters outside the Swedish west coast are among the busiest ones in the world.  The habitat in the region is partly highly exploited and affected by boat transport. The assessment is set to medium due to the high rate of transports in parts of the habitat.
F07. Disturbance and noise from jets, boating, kayaking, water skiing etc.  The assessment for the pressure and threat is set as medium as the effects are direct on a medium scale.
F08. Physical disturbance; exploitation (bridges, marinas, cabling etc.) dredging of fairways and dumping of dredged spoils are directly affecting vegetation and benthic organisms but also indirectly by shading and by degrading water quality (resuspension of sediments). The result of the pressure analysis shows high impact (30%) on the habitat therefore is the assessment set to high and also the threat due to continued pressure.   
F20: Activities resulting in discharge of toxic substances such as leakage of toxic hull antifouling (TBT) despite long term ban. There are also signs of general deterioration of fish health (e.g.  Zoarces viviparus)  the reason is unknown, but exposure to unknown toxic substances are indicated.  Point sources from sewage plants and also diffuse leakage of sewer from individual households and summer cottages due to substandard sewage treatment is problematic in certain  coastal areas causing nutrient  (nitrogen) load causing decreased light penetration affecting macrophytic production.  The pressure and threat is set as medium due to the ecological effects of the pressure's as well as threat’s direct impact.  
F22. Disposal from residential and recreational activities generating micro- macro- particulate pollution as a source for marine litter. The habitat is sensitive with quite low turnover rate therefore the assessment is set as medium for both pressure and threat. 
G01.  In Swedish coastal areas has the pressure from recreational fishing significant impact on fish populations (SLU aqua). But in offshore areas the pressure from commercial fishing is also of importance on fish populations (e.g. cod). The harvesting of fish resulting in a shortage of large carnivorous fish. This leads to top down effects strengthening the impacts of eutrophication leading to decreased habitat quality for example increased production of filamentous algae competing with eelgrass and other macrophytes. Also threatening certain targeted species and populations for instance cod. During the reporting period measures have been taken limiting the fishing pressure, however  the results from this differs geographically,  In southern Kattegat the populations are increasing but this is not the case further north. The fishing pressures outside the restricted areas are still unsustainable for some species (e.g. cod). The pressure is therefore set as high and the threat as medium due to expected improvement during coming reporting periods. However, due to the uncertain results of the measures (the lack of population recover in Bohuslän) there is a need for more knowledge.
Xu: Decrease in blue mussel distribution has been reported along the West coast of Sweden and Norway with potential   effects on mussel associated fauna e.g. mussel eating organisms. The cause of the decrease are not fully understood and therefore set as unknown. The assessment of the threat is set as high due to the unknown consequences for the habitat.

featurename: Sandbanks which are slightly covered by sea water all the time
pressures_info:
A20. Eutrophication is less pronounced offshore but in inshore waters and especially for seagrass beds it causes less light penetration and risk of overgrowth of filamentous algae, which effects the seagrass beds. Historical data from the Swedish West Coast (1930ies) indicate that eelgrass was found down to 5-7 meters in fiords and 8-10 m in open waters compared to todays' 2-3 m and 4-5 m respectively. Illustrating a large area (50%) loss due to light penetration.  Due to measures for decreasing the nutrient load from agriculture water quality improvements has been observed but the conditions are still not sufficient. Agriculture is the largest contributor of anthropogenic nutrient load to marine waters. This load is entering the coast and sea through riverine outlets.  Kattegat is a major national recipient of anthropogenic nutrients (31%). About 50% of the total anthropogenic nitrogen load to the Swedish west coast is from agriculture and for phosphorus 46%. Due to guidelines this pressure (A20) should be used also when the source (natural or artificial) is unknown.  The assessment medium is set due to the pressure having mainly indirect influence or acting on regionally areas.
E02. The waters outside the Swedish west coast are among the busiest ones in the world. The assessment is set to medium due to the ecosystem sensitivity (especially seabirds and plankton) to oil spill and the high frequency of ships generating noise pollution and disturbance.
F08.  Physical disturbance;  exploitation  (bridges, marinas, cabling), dredging  of fairways and dumping of dredged spoils are  directly affecting eelgrass beds (Zostera) but also indirectly by shading and degraded water quality due to boating activities (resuspension of sediments) . The assessment is set to medium due to the pressure acting regionally on Zostera beds (Moksnes et al. 2018).
F20: Activities resulting in discharge of toxic substances such as leakage of toxic hull antifouling (TBT) despite long term ban. There are also signs of general health deterioration of fish health (e.g.  Zoarces viviparus) the reason is unknown exposure to unknown toxic substances are indicated.  Point sources from sewage plants (Kattegat) and also diffuse leakage of sewer from individual households and summer cottages due to substandard sewage treatment is problematic in certain coastal areas causing nutrient (nitrogen) load.  Which might effect for example seagrass beds due to decreased light. The pressure is set as medium due to the uncertain linkage to the specific habitat however acting on a large scale. 
G01. In Swedish coastal areas has the pressure from recreational fishing significant impact on fish populations (SLU aqua). But in offshore areas the pressure from commercial fishing is also of importance. The harvesting of fish resulting in a shortage of large carnivorous fish. This leads to top down effects strengthening the impacts of eutrophication leading to decreased habitat quality for example increased production of filamentous algae competing with eelgrass and other macrophytes. Also threatening certain targeted species and populations for instance cod and spur-dog. During the reporting period measures have been taken limiting the fishing pressure, however  the results from this differs geographically,  In southern Kattegat the populations are increasing but this is not the case further north. The fishing pressures outside the restricted areas are still unsustainable for some species (e.g. cod). The pressure is therefore set as high and the threat as medium due to expected improvement during coming reporting periods.

G03. Bottom trawling are disturbing habitats direct or indirect by sedimentation and resuspension. Pressure is set as high during the reporting period according to the results from the physical disturbance analysis showing the pressure acting over large areas of the habitat (21%). Threat is set as medium due to measures taken on restricting bottom trawling being implemented during the reporting period.

featurename: Mudflats and sandflats not covered by seawater at low tide
pressures_info:
A20. Eutrophication effects due to high loads of agricultural nutrients to coastal waters causes less light penetration and risk of overgrowth of filamentous algae, which effects the vegetation. Due to measures for decreasing the nutrient load from agriculture, water quality improvements has been observed but the conditions are still not sufficient. According to guidelines this pressure (A20) should be used also when the source (natural or artificial) is unknown. The assessment medium is set due to the pressure having moderate influence in the habitat. The threat is set to medium as the improvement of the measures is expected to be slow.
F08. Physical disturbance; exploitation, dredging of fairways and dumping of dredged spoils are directly affecting vegetation and benthic organisms but also indirectly by shading and by degrading water quality (resuspension of sediments). The result of the pressure analysis shows high impact (30%) on the habitat therefore is the assessment set to high and also the threat due to continued pressure.   
 F20: Activities resulting in discharge of toxic substances such as leakage of toxic hull antifouling (TBT) despite long term ban. There are also signs of general deterioration of fish health (e.g.  Zoarces viviparus)  the reason is unknown, but exposure to unknown toxic substances are indicated.  Point sources from sewage plants (Kattegat) and also diffuse leakage of sewer from individual households and summer cottages due to substandard sewage treatment is problematic in certain coastal areas causing nutrient (nitrogen) load causing decreased light penetration affecting macrophytic production.  On the biogeographical level 25% of the anthropogenic nitrogen load and 34% of the anthropogenic phosphorus is originated residential and recreational sewage in total (SMEDS).  The pressure and threat is set as medium due to the exploitation rate within the habitat and the ecological effects of the pressure's as well as threats direct impact.  
F22. Disposal from residential and recreational activities generating micro- macro- particulate pollution as a source for marine litter. The habitat is shallow and close to shore and therefore sensitive for particulate pollution, so the assessment is set as medium for both pressure and threat. 
I02. The Invasive Pacific oyster (Crassostrea gigas) has established along the Swedish west coast and the mud - and sandflats have locally been affected heavily. The c gigas forms reefs that changes the condition of the habitat and may also compete for substrate with native species (e.g. O. edulis, M. edulis). The assessment of the pressure and threat is set as medium.

featurename: Submarine structures made by leaking gases
pressures_info:
G03 This pressure is of significance for pockmarks and not for bubbling reefs.  Bottom trawling is affecting unprotected pockmarks direct (trawling in or close to habitat) or indirect by sedimentation and resuspension.  The assessment is set as high as the pressure is acting on a large scale.  Implemented restrictions have limited effects due to the distribution of pockmarks in relation to areas of restriction.  Threat is set due continuous pressure, however there is a need of improved habitat knowledge. 
N05: Climate change resulting in changes in marine pH and potential acidification effects. Acidification can impact on the carbonate structures of the habitat.  Assessed as medium threat.

featurename: Tilio-Acerion forests of slopes, screes and ravines
pressures_info:
Usage of not considerate methods in forestry. Non-harmonized forestry and nature protection legislation. Not accepting nature protection requirements.
Inadequate forestry practices resulted in the loss of native tree species.
In the past this habitat has often been spared from forestry activities, which is no longer the case and renewal logging is also planned in these sensitive stands.
On a larger part of this forest habitat, it would be appropriate to stop forest management to maintain or improve its status.

pressures_info:
"Pressões: Uma vez que os indivíduos que ocorrem na Região não frequentam exclusivamente a ZEE dos Açores mas pertencem a uma população mais ampla (população do Atlântico Norte), que se movimenta num território oceânico único, optou-se por fazer a análise das ameaças à viabilidade da espécie a longo prazo a uma escala global. 

No passado, a espécie foi caçada intensamente e embora não existam estimativas fiáveis do efectivo populacional actual, existem evidências de que a população recuperou. Presentemente a população atinge algumas centenas de milhar de indivíduos e apresenta uma distribuição vasta. Apesar disto, a caça, que se mantém até aos dias de hoje, continua a constituir uma ameaça à viabilidade da espécie a longo prazo. Em diversas regiões ao longo da sua área de distribuição, a mortalidade acidental em artes de pesca e a colisão com embarcações constituem ameaças significativas à viabilidade da espécie a longo prazo. 



Ameaç: 
Não é expectável que as ameaças actuais deixem de actuar sobre a espécie num futuro próximo.

A actividade de observação comercial de cetáceos poderá causar alterações comportamentais nos indivíduos, embora não deva constituir uma fonte de ameaça à viabilidade da espécie a longo prazo. A espécie não ocorre regularmente nos Açores, pelo que não deverá ser alvo de perturbação significativa. No entanto, na maior parte das regiões onde a espécie ocorre não existe legislação que regulamenta a observação comercial de cetáceos, pelo que a população poderá estar sujeita a esta ameaça a nível global.
"

pressures_info: Results from the survey above shows that churches with bat colonies had decreased significantly in frequency from 61% in 1980s to 38% by 2016. All abandoned churches had been fitted with flood-lights in the period between the two surveys. The loss of bat colonies from lit churches was highly significant and most obvious when lights were applied from all directions, leaving no dark corridor for the bats to leave and return to the roost. In contrast, in churches that were not lit, all of 13 bat colonies remained after 25+ years between the. This indicates that light is can be problem.
pressures_source: Rydell, J., Eklöf, J., Sánchez-Navarro, S. 2017. Age of enlightenment: long-term effects of outdoor aesthetic lights on bats in churches. R.Soc. open sci. 4: 161077

pressures_info: The beetle occur only at sites with even higher density of dead wood, >30 m3/ha. Generally, such high volumes of logs occur only in pristine old-growth forests, or in semi natural forests where some selective logging may have taken place in the past. This species is therefore threatened by intensive forestry management, in particular forestry clearance and the removal of dead and dying trees. It is also negatively threatened by drainage of forest. Overgrazing by elks also reduce future aspen trees.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info: The pressure is intensive forestry management which have to low concentrations of dead wood. This species is therefore threatened by intensive forestry management, in particular forestry clearance and the removal of dead and dying trees. Lack of natural disturbance like forest fires, flooding is probably a threat. Also removal of dead trees after disturbance like storms.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info: The species is threatened by ceased grazing, mainly because the old trees suffer from competition and shading from the younger ones. Many places also lack trees to be the next generation of suitable substrate. This will result in a gap in time when no suitable substrate is available. Forestry also isolate occurrences by low consideration, i.e. leaving large enough areas with suitable substrate and leaving trees that can be future substrate.

Limiting pollution is one of the resons for seal recovery over time.
Bycatch is not limiting to the population, but still a problem despite active measures in place.
Competition for food is the problems with fiseries.

pressures_info:
Blue whales may be subject to a number of local and/or regional environmental pressures and threats throughout their North Atlantic range and in Irish offshore waters. In the development and preparation of the Conservation Plan for Cetaceans in Irish waters (DEHLG, 2009), a comprehensive review of the pressures and threats believed and/or documented to be acting on this species and/or its functional group was undertaken.
The main pressures thought to be acting on this species in Irish waters are considered to involve commercial shipping-based activities that occur primarily on a local or regional scale and/or on a temporary or intermittent basis (e.g., impacts arising from shipping movements or geophysical seismic exploration; DEHLG, 2009).
Since current blue whale distribution is likely to be offshore Atlantic in nature, where a pressure may be regionally intensive the ranking given is one of medium importance due also to the species’ depleted population status.
It should be noted that in relation to seismic exploration, which tends to occur on a local or occasionally regional scale in the waters of Ireland's EEZ and is comparatively low in coverage relative to several other member states, a robust regulatory and management regime applies in order to avoid potentially significant impacts on all species of marine mammal.
This regime takes cognisance of recent scientific research and surveillance findings (e.g., Berrow et al., 2018; Rogan et al., 2018) and it is expected that this practice will continue into the future.
There is no evidence to suggest a change in the main pressures thought to be acting on this species in the near future (i.e., threats). However surveillance of the species and the pressures potentially acting upon it will continue, while the application of strong management measures (e.g., via the statutory/regulatory process) to avoid potentially significant impacts is also expected to continue.

pressures_info:
Northern bottlenose whales may be subject to local and/or regional environmental pressures and threats throughout their North Atlantic range and in Irish inshore/offshore waters. In the development and preparation of the Conservation Plan for Cetaceans in Irish waters (DEHLG, 2009), a comprehensive review of the pressures and threats believed and/or documented to be acting on this species and/or its functional group was undertaken.
The main pressures thought to be acting on this species in Irish waters are considered to involve commercial shipping-based or vessel-based activities that occur primarily on a local or regional scale and/or on a temporary or intermittent basis (e.g., impacts arising from shipping movements or geophysical seismic exploration; DEHLG, 2009).
Among the potential pressures that may act on this species is the use of military sonars in the deeper ocean and adjacent continental margins which, while not employed by the Irish Naval Service, is known and documented to occur in the waters of Ireland’s EEZ (e.g., McCauley, 2015; Berrow et al., 2018). Such anthropogenic sound sources have been implicated previously in a number of beaked whale stranding events (DAHG, 2014) and questions remain with regard to an unusual series of strandings of this and other beaked whale species in western Ireland, the UK and adjacent states in August-September 2018 (DCHG, 2018 unpublished data; IWDG, 2018 unpublished data).
Since Northern bottlenose whale distribution is known to be primarily offshore in the deeper Atlantic Ocean, where there is a documented pressure that may be regionally intensive the ranking given is one of medium importance, given relatively low population estimates for the species (Cañadas et al., 2011; see also 6.2 above) and also uncertainty with regard to the species’ movements and population/breeding structure (Taylor et al., 2008).
It should be noted that in relation to seismic exploration, which tends to occur on a local or occasionally regional scale in the waters of Ireland's EEZ and is comparatively low in coverage relative to several other member states, a robust regulatory and management regime applies in order to avoid potentially significant impacts on all species of marine mammal.
This regime takes cognisance of recent scientific research and surveillance findings (e.g., Baines et al., 2013; McCauley, 2015; Frouin-Mouy et al., 2017; Hammond et al., 2017; Kavanagh et al., 2017; Rogan et al., 2017; O’Donnell et al., 2018; Berrow et al., 2018; Rogan et al., 2018) and it is expected that this practice will continue into the future.
There is no evidence to suggest a change in the main pressures thought to be acting on this species in the near future (i.e., threats). However surveillance of the species and the pressures potentially acting upon it will continue, while the application of strong management measures (e.g., via the statutory/regulatory process) to avoid potentially significant impacts is also expected to continue.

Threats: Een grote verandering in de komende periode op de Noordzee (migratiegebied, foerageergebied) is de sterke groei van het aantal offshore wind activiteiten. Wat daarvan het (cumulatieve) effect is op zeehonden is nog onbekend. 
Ook zandextractie kan een probleem vormen.

pressures_info:
A20, A21 - This species is particularly sensitive to water quality and is often used as an indicator species. Excessive use of chemicals, namely fertilizers and pesticides, for agricultural production, can affect the small watercourses where this species occurs.
D02 - The construction of dams or mini-dams causes the conversion of a lotic to lentic system, unsuitable for the survival of the species. The construction of some dams in the area of occurrence of the species (Sabor, Tâmega) is responsible for the reduction of available habitat.
A30, A33, K04 - Interventions on natural watercourses and their banks - such as canalization for irrigation, clearing of vegetation, inert extraction - alter the structure of their habitat, and may lead to the disappearance of the species locally. The extent and frequency of vegetation clearing can be a serious threat to the conservation of the species if it does not take into account its life cycle, which may take two years to complete. On the other hand, the excessive vegetation causes the shading of the water lines (the species seems to have preference for watercourses exposed to sunlight) and affects the dispersal of the species. Extensive agriculture and livestock favor this type of habitat.
A06, A09 - cessation of grazing or overgrazing can both be inadvisable for promoting densification of vegetation or, on the other hand, its excessive consumption and trampling. Extensive grazing allows the vegetation structure to be maintained at a level suitable for the maintenance of the species.
N01, N02  - Extreme droughts are expected to increase in number and intensity in the near future due to temperature changes and decreases in precipitation, with significant impacts on species habitat. mainly in central and southern Portugal.

pressures_info:
Pressões: 
A espécie é particularmente vulnerável aos traumas acústicos, que poderão ser provocados pela utilização de sonares activos de alta intensidade para fins científicos e militares, e de cargas explosivas subaquáticas. 

A espécie é pouco conspícua e difícil de observar, pelo que a população não deverá ser alvo de perturbação por parte da actividade de observação comercial de observação de cetáceos.

Ameaças: 
Embora exista uma Directiva Comunitária que exorta os Estados Membros a criarem restrições ao uso de sonares activos de alta intensidade, ainda não existe legislação que regulamente a utilização deste tipo de equipamento. Além do mais, tendo em conta que o efeito destes dois tipos de ameaça se pode fazer sentir a grandes distâncias, os indivíduos desta espécie poderão ser afectados mesmo quando a ameaça se localiza fora da ZEE dos Açores. Deste modo, não é expectável que as ameaças actuais deixem de actuar num futuro próximo. O aumento do tráfego marítimo é outra fonte de poluição sonora que poderá vir a ameaçar a viabilidade da espécie a longo prazo.
