Eutrophication is the most serious threat for this habitat type. Measures to reduce its effects have been taken during the last decades and especially during the last few years to reduce nutrient loads from the watershed area. The reduced amount of nutrients in seawater results in the restoration of structural and functional features of this habitat type while simultaneously restoring typical species composition.

pressures_info: Influences négatives : aménagement des berges, modification du fonctionnement hydrologique, changement de niveau trophique, invasions biologiques, destruction de l'habitat (aménagements ou plantation d'arbres)

Diverses sources d'altération du fonctionnement hydrologique ou hydrogéologique et de pollution des eaux d'alimentation des nappes ou des sols, résultat des activités humaines, sont les principales causes de la dégradation de l'habitat, en plus des destructions directes ou indirectes par des opérations de drainage, pour la mise en culture, la plantation de peupliers ou autres feuillus, et parfois encore pour des aménagements de loisirs ou l'urbanisation. 
L'habitat peut également évoluer de manière naturelle vers d'autres types d'habitats, par exemple par envasement/atterrissement progressif.

featurename: Inland dunes with open Corynephorus and Agrostis grasslands
pressures_info: Dans l'ensemble, les atteintes récentes et menaces les plus importantes sont liées à la fragmentation de l'habitat, à sa sensibilité à l'envahissement par des espèces exogènes et à la dynamique de stabilisation / fermeture de ces pelouses pionnières. D'autres pressions et menaces dépendent de la situation géomorphologique de l'habitat (en vallée fluviale : modification du fonctionnement hydrodynamique, extraction de sables alluviaux)

The pressures and threats presented in Section 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium or low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact. Data were gathered from the source listed in Section 7.2. For the current reporting period data on pressures were available for four 2150 sites surveyed by Martin (2018d) that represent 99.9% of the known area of the habitat within Ireland.
Only three pressures were recorded on more than one occasion and all three are listed as medium-importance pressures in Section 7.1.
The one medium-importance pressure recorded during the current reporting period which was not recorded in the previous recording period (NPWS, 2013) was F07 Sports, tourism and leisure activities. There were five medium/high-importance pressures recorded during the previous reporting period (NPWS, 2013) that were not considered to be of medium/high-importance during the current reporting period. Two of these, A02 Conversion from one type of agricultural land use to another (excluding drainage and burning) and J04 Mixed source soil pollution and solid waste (excluding discharges), were not recorded during the current reporting period. Two more, I02 Other invasive alien species (other than species of Union concern) and CA03 Maintain existing extensive agricultural practices and agricultural landscape features, were recorded but as neutral or positive impacts. The final one, L02 Abiotic natural processes (e.g. erosion, silting up, drying out, submersion, salinization), was recorded as A06 during the current reporting period.
Pressures and threats noted less frequently and/or that impacted only small areas were deemed to be of low importance. Low-importance pressures recorded during the current reporting period included C06 Dumping/depositing of inert materials from terrestrial extraction, F09 Deposition and treatment of waste/garbage from household/recreational facilities, and H08 Other human intrusions and disturbance not mentioned above; this code was used to record accidental burning of the dune heath.
Climate change has not been added as an impact but it is likely to affect the range of 2150 over the coming decades. The short-term predictability of the effect of climate change and in particular the resultant mean sea level rise and extreme weather events is unknown but the gradual long-term trend will see an increase in mean sea level and extreme weather (EPA, 2017) which will affect the 2150 habitat.
The three pressures presented in Section 7.1 are also considered to be the three main threats in the future. In the previous reporting period F08 Modification of coastline, estuary and coastal conditions for development, use and protection of residential, commercial, industrial and recreational infrastructure and areas (including sea defence or coast protection works and infrastructures) was considered to be a threat, but during the current reporting period it was not considered to be a threat as it was not applicable for the areas within the sites where the 2150 habitat was recorded.

featurename: Oligotrophic to mesotrophic standing waters with vegetation of the Littorelletea uniflorae and/or of the Isoëto-Nanojuncetea
pressures_info:
Pressures and threats were recorded in the field, as well as through examination of aerial imagery for the catchments of lakes with Mixed Najas flexilis habitat (3130) (Roden and Murphy, 2014; Roden et al., 2016, 2017, 2018, in prep.). Data were available for 50 x 3130 lake catchments.
K04 and K05 were used for the drainage pressure that lowered water levels at Kiltooris, leading to the conservation condition of the habitat declining to Bad at one of the most important 3130 and Najas flexilis lakes in Ireland.
Drainage (K04, K05), associated with agriculture, forestry and turf cutting, was a significant pressure in at least five other catchments.
Agriculture (A26, A25) was noted as a pressure at 30 (60%) of the 3130 lake catchments examined.
F12 covers discharges from both urban waste-water (UWW) treatment plants (sewered) and on-site systems (unsewered). On-site systems are present in at least 31 (62%) of catchments examined, whereas UWW discharges may be present in 6 (12%).
Competition from Elodea canadensis (I02) was noted as impacting at two sites (Sessiagh and Port). Introduction of this non-native species may work in combination with eutrophication, and its impact (invasive response) may not be long term. Impacts were also indicated at three more lakes (Nageltia, Tully and Kindrum), but these may be recovering from the initial Elodea canadensis invasion.
Forestry (B23) was noted as a pressure in 14 (28%) of 3130 lake catchments examined.
Turf cutting (C05) was noted as a pressure at 4 catchments (8%).
Golf courses were present adjacent to at least two 3130 lakes, therefore F16 ‘Other residential and recreational activities and structures generating diffuse pollution to surface or ground waters’ can be considered a pressure and threat of low importance.
There are abstractions from at least three of the lakes (Knocka, Shannagh and Ballynakill); however there is no evidence that these are currently impacting on the habitat. It is possible, however, that abstractions may be a threat, in combination with predicted droughts owing to climate change.
Owing to their likely continuation, all pressures were also listed as threats.
No genuine changes in pressures or threats were identified. Both the reporting form and Article 17 pressures and threats list have changed since Article 17 2013.

featurename: Semi-natural dry grasslands and scrubland facies on calcareous substrates (Festuco-Brometalia) (* important orchid sites)
pressures_info:
Pressures and threats were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium or low), frequency of occurrence and area of impact, using the data gathered during the 2015-17 GMS and presented in Martin et al. (2018) The three pressures reported in Table 4 of Martin et al. (2018) that contributed to the largest areas of 6210/*6210 loss are listed above in Section 7.1 as the three high-importance pressures. Two of the three high-importance pressures were also frequently recorded impacts within extant areas of the habitat (Table 9 of Martin et al., 2018), with A02 recorded in Table 9 under ‘agricultural intensification’ and A10 recorded under ‘species composition change’ and ‘abandonment, lack of grazing’. The two medium-importance pressures I02 and I04 listed above in Section 7.1 were the only other impacts within Table 9 that were recorded more than once with a high/medium intensity, and based on expert opinion both are considered to be medium-importance pressures at a national scale.
Curtis & Wilson (2014) recorded pressures at an additional twelve 6210/*6210 sites and A10 recorded under ‘abandonment, lack of grazing’ was the most frequently recorded pressure, recorded as high intensity at six sites and medium intensity at two sites. In addition to the GMS data presented on I02 and I04 these pressures were also each recorded at one site by Curtis & Wilson (2014), with I02 recorded once as a high-intensity pressure and I04 once as a medium-intensity pressure. Although intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock (A09) was not recorded within the 6210/*6210 habitat by the GMS it was recorded by Curtis & Wilson (2014) at two sites, once as a high-intensity pressure and once as a medium-intensity pressure, and it has therefore been included as a third medium-importance pressure.
The problematic native species listed under I04 was Pteridium aquilinum (Bracken) and the most commonly recorded invasive species listed under I02 was Cotoneaster integrifolius.
Further research on the impacts of climate change on this habitat is required.  Impacts may affect the range and species composition of the habitat in the future but it is difficult to determine what level of importance should be assigned at this stage (EPA, 2017).
The six pressures presented in Section 7.1 are also considered to be the six main threats in the future.

featurename: Mediterranean and thermo-Atlantic halophilous scrubs (Sarcocornetea fruticosi)
pressures_info:
Pressures and threats in section 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium or low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact, using the data gathered during the 2017-18 saltmarsh monitoring survey and presented in Brophy et al. (in prep.), as well as reviewing other sources of information. 
All pressures presented in NPWS (2013) were considered for the current reporting period. Of the eight high or medium-ranked pressures in NPWS (2013), none were recorded for the current reporting period. Invasive non-native species (previously I01, now I02 under the new impact recording scheme) was previously ranked as being of low importance; however, for the current reporting period, it has been raised to medium importance. This is due to the fact that Spartina anglica may out-compete Sarcocornia perennis for light and resources and any evidence that Spartina anglica does not out-compete Sarcocornia perennis in Irish saltmarshes appears to be anecdotal. Previous research suggests that coverage of a range of saltmarsh vegetation communities declines as Spartina anglica spreads (Nehring and Hesse, 2008). The only pressure of high importance recorded affecting 1420 for the current reporting period was J02 Mixed source marine water pollution (marine and coastal). This took the form of algal mats, which had a high-intensity impact at two sites and led to a loss of 1420 habitat. A09 Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock was noted at Ballyteige Burrow during the SAMFHIRES project (P. Perrin, pers. comm.), but this was not considered to be affecting 1420. Of the other pressures recorded in the previous reporting period, species composition change (succession) (previously K02.01, now L02 under the new impact recording scheme) was not assessed as it is considered a natural process. Similarly, Erosion (previously K01.01, now under L01) was considered a natural process and so was not assessed. The remaining pressures recorded in the previous reporting period were not noted during the 2017-18 survey. For some, this may be that they were not occurring at the sites surveyed, while others are difficult to define and assess, such as diffuse household pollution (H01.08).
Climate change has not been added as an impact of medium or high importance, but it is likely to affect the range of 1420 over the coming decades. The long-term trend will see an increase in mean sea level and extreme weather events (Desmond et al., 2017), which will impact the formation of the 1420 habitat.
The two pressures presented in section 7.1 are also considered to be the two main threats into the future.
pressures_source:
Pressures and threats were selected and ranked from the following data:
Brophy, J.T. et al., (in prep.) Saltmarsh Monitoring Project 2017-2018: Final Report. Irish Wildlife Manuals No. 1XX. National Parks and Wildlife Service, Department of Culture, Heritage and the Gaeltacht, Dublin.
McCorry, M.J. and Ryle, T. (2009) Saltmarsh Monitoring Project 2007-2008: Final report. An unpublished report for the National Parks and Wildlife Service, Department of Environment, Heritage and Local Government, Dublin.
National Parks and Wildlife Service (2013). The Status of Protected EU Habitats and Species in Ireland. Overview Volume 1. Unpublished Report. National Parks and Wildlife Services. Department of Arts, Heritage and the Gaeltacht, Dublin.

featurename: Juniperus communis formations on heaths or calcareous grasslands
pressures_info:
Pressures and threats were ranked from data gathered during the 2017 monitoring programme and further information on the occurrence and frequency of pressures can be found in O’Neill & Martin (2017).
No pressures were identified as being of high or medium importance nationally. Any high or medium-impact pressures recorded during surveys usually affected only small areas of the habitat, or occurred at a low frequency across the sites surveyed.
Pressures that affected relatively large areas of the sites where they occurred, but which occurred at a low frequency across the sites surveyed, include A09 Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock and A06 Abandonment of grassland management (e.g. cessation of grazing or of mowing). In most cases where grazing was recorded, it was regarded as either beneficial or not damaging to the habitat. The issue of grazing is complex and a balance needs to be maintained between the beneficial effects (e.g. creating bare patches for seedling recruitment and prevention of unwanted scrub encroachment) and the negative effects (such trampling or browsing of developing seedlings). Grazing is discussed in more detail in O’Neill & Martin (2017).
Erosion was recorded at several sites but was not listed as a pressure nationally. Juniper formations surveyed in 2017 were seen to persist even in the presence of severe erosion, and juniper density was often found to be highest in areas where outcropping rock was freely interspersed through bare peat or heathy vegetation. In some cases the cause of erosion was not clear and could have been either natural (through exposure) or because of past overgrazing. Future monitoring surveys should help to elucidate the long-term effects of erosion on juniper formations. Erosion in the context of 5130 habitat is discussed further in O’Neill & Martin (2017).
Pressures that were high-impact but which affected only a small area of the habitat at a local level include A05 Removal of small landscape features for agricultural land parcel consolidation (in the form of juniper scrub removal), A11 Burning for agriculture, I02 Other invasive alien species (other than species of Union concern), and F01 Conversion from other land uses to housing, settlement or recreational areas (excluding drainage and modification of coastline, estuary and coastal conditions).
One formation was reduced in area by road construction, so the pressure E01 Roads, paths railroads and related infrastructure (e.g. bridges, viaducts, tunnels) operated locally but the area affected was of a low significance nationally.
Periodic flooding from turloughs or lakes (which led to the death of juniper shrubs and some area loss) is not listed as an impact. It is difficult to quantify the long-term effect of this occurrence as it is likely to be one that the juniper habitat has had to adapt to over many decades. Further monitoring and research will be required to determine if such episodic occurrences are damaging or, in fact, ultimately beneficial by invigorating the formation through the creation of seed germination niches and thus promoting seedling recruitment.
Climate change has not been added as an impact but it is likely to affect the altitudinal and latitudinal range of 5130 in Ireland in decades to come if average temperatures continue to rise (juniper seed germination is linked to cold temperatures, and juniper seedlings are susceptible to drought; Thomas et al., 2007).
pressures_source: Pressures and threats were recorded during 2017 field surveys (O’Neill & Martin, 2017).

featurename: Hydrophilous tall herb fringe communities of plains and of the montane to alpine levels
pressures_info:
IAS union concern :  Impatiens glandulifera Royle; Threskiornis aethiopicus Latham, 1790; Trachemys scripta Schoepff, 1792; Vespa velutina nigrithorax de Buysson, 1905;
Pressures and threats presented in 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium, low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact where data were available. Data gathered from the sources listed in Section 7.2 are summarised in the backing document ‘1601_NSUH17_IMPACTS’. For the current reporting period, data on pressures were available from two sources – Galway County Council (2017) and Martin et al. (2018). Habitat 6430 was recorded at the two NSUH sites, Slieve Mish Mountains SAC (002185) and Caha Mountains SAC (000093) (Perrin et al. 2014a, 2014b), however they were not assessed and therefore no impacts were recorded. Data from a shapefile on locations of wildfires, which was provided by NPWS, were also utilised, with relevant data for 6430 habitat located at Kilgarrylander within the Slieve Mish Mountains. As the data from the current reporting period only represent a small proportion of the data collected to inform NPWS (2013a), the pressures and threats information presented in NPWS (2013a) were called upon to inform the current reporting period.
All pressures presented in NPWS (2013a) were considered for the current reporting period. For one of these there was additional evidence for its importance provided by the data collected during the current reporting period. Using the crosswalk referenced in the backing document ‘1601_NSUH17_IMPACTS’ to convert old impact codes to the new impact recording scheme, this pressure crosswalked to A09 (Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock) (recorded once but no effect was given). Based on evidence collected over the last two reporting periods and expert opinion, A09 was retained as a medium-importance pressure. There were not enough new data collected in the current reporting period to downgrade the pressure A09, mainly caused by cattle in the lowland riparian community and by sheep in the upland community.
A31 (Drainage for use as agricultural land) was recorded as a low-importance pressure in the previous reporting period. In the current reporting period there have been three documented losses of area (two full and one partial) due to agricultural improvements caused by drainage. Based on evidence from the current reporting period and expert opinion, A31 was upgraded to a medium-importance pressure.
Both I01 (Invasive alien species of Union concern) in the form of Impatiens glandulifera and I02 (Other invasive alien species (other than species of Union concern) in the form of Epilobium brunnescens were recorded in the previous reporting period under the old code I01 as a medium-importance pressure. There were not enough new data collected in the current reporting period to amend these pressures and they were therefore retained as medium-importance pressures.
The low-importance impacts A02 (Conversion from one type of agricultural land use to another (excluding drainage and burning)), A26 (Agricultural activities generating diffuse pollution to surface or ground waters) and J03 (Mixed source air pollution, air-borne pollutants), recorded in NPWS (2013a) but not in this reporting period, were retained as low-importance pressures for the current reporting period. A11 (Burning for agriculture) and L02 (Natural succession resulting in species composition change (other than by direct changes of agricultural or forestry practices)), recorded within the current reporting period only, were also assessed as low importance for 6430 habitat due to low frequency and area impacted upon. It should be noted however that the medium-importance pressure A31 (Drainage for use as agricultural land) is likely to cause an increased frequency in the pressure L02 so this may be ranked as a higher importance pressure in future reporting periods.
The four medium-importance pressures reported in Section 7.1 are also considered to be the main threats to the 6430 habitat in the future.

pressures_info:
B03 - replacement of native vegetation, often associated with afforestation of large areas of monocultures with non-indigenous species, with destruction of the marginal zones where the host plants supporting the caterpillars are found.
I02 - The introduction or expansion of non-native invasive plants creates situations of competition, excluding native E. aurinia host plants.
H08 - Fire acts on lepidopterans on two levels: direct destruction of individuals and destruction of host plants.
K02 - The drainage and landfill of wetlands, usually for urban development, construction of infrastructure or installation of new agricultural areas, destroys the habitats of this species, causing fragmentation and isolation of populations.
A20, A21 - excessive use of chemicals, namely fertilizers and pesticides, for agricultural production, which can lead to the reduction of host plants of this species..
A09 - intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock causes damage to vegetation reducing nectar-producing plants,  affecting host plants 
A15 - Cutting vegetation at inappropriate times, if carried out during the larval-chrysalis development period, may lead to the disappearance of the species at local level.
N01, N02  - Extreme droughts are expected to increase in number and intensity in the near future due to temperature changes and decreases in precipitation, with significant impacts on species habitat.

pressures_source: Moço, G., Serrano, E., Guerreiro, M., Ferreira, A. F., Petrucci-Fonseca, F., Santana, D., ... & Pérez, J. M. (2014). Does livestock influence the diet of Iberian ibex Capra pyrenaica in the Peneda-Gerês National Park (Portugal)?. Mammalia, 78(3), 393-399.

pressures_info: There is no species action plan for this butterfly in Sweden. This species is not believed to face major threats other than fragmentation of known population. No conservation measures needed today.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

Beside the pressures responsible for the earlier declines some new ones have faced during the 21th century: a warmer climate/droughts and increase of short term regulation in rivers used for hydro power. These stressors are affecting M. margaritifera in all regions in Sweden.

pressures_info: The lists of pressures and threats are based on expert judgements and on published sources. Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), and more knowledge and data.

The species habitat is old-growth spruce forests close to the tree border. It is therefore threatened by forestry management, in particular forestry clearance and even the removal of selected trees. Reduction of the habitat of the species is mainly due to forestry and maybe also by grazing effects of reindeers.
Climate change is in the future expected to affect the species habitat in a negative direction. This is an assumption based on forecast for climate change and effects on alpine forest close to tree-line.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), as well as more knowledge and data. Abandonment of grassland management mainly occurred before the last 12 years period but the effects are still a problem, especially at the river Tore älv.

pressures_info: 1) L01 'Abiotic natural processes' is used for the decrease in aeolian activity which is essential for the new development of the habitat type. pressures_info:
The pressures detected and communicated in previous sexennial reports are becoming less relevant, due to the application of the Natura 2000 sites management plans,  in which this hábitat is mostly located. Therefore, and since the category of less importance (L) has been deleted, it is considered that there are no pressures noteworthy. In relation to the threats, the main ones for this habitat are those that appear in any of the predicted climate change scenarios (see http://climaimpacto.eu/efectos/escenarios-climaticos-en-canarias/), although the uncertainty is still too high to be considered as threats of medium importance.

On the other hand, the existing fire extinguishing system makes fire (codes A11, H04 and M09) to be considered a threat of only minor importance

pressures_info: Cet habitat a souffert de nombreuses atteintes par le passé (déprise pastorale, modification des dynamiques fluviales, extraction de granulats, urbanisation, infrastructure touristique, disparition des lapins...). Dans l'ensemble, les atteintes récentes et menaces les plus importantes sont liées à la fragmentation de l'habitat et à la dynamique de stabilisation / fermeture de ces pelouses pionnières. D'autres pressions et menaces dépendent de la situation géomorphologique de l'habitat (en vallée fluviale : modification du fonctionnement hydrodynamique, extraction de sables alluviaux).

pressures_info: De façon générale les pressions et menaces consistent en diverses pollutions pouvant s'accumuler avec les laisses de mer, modification du biotope soit par impact direct (extraction de matériau, aménagements) soit par impact indirect (nettoyage des plages, surfréquentation) et réponse aux changements globaux notamment du niveau de la mer et du rythme des tempêtes.

Influences négatives : modification des conditions hydrologique et de la qualité physico-chimiques des eaux suite à des aménagements (routes, urbanisation, installations agricoles), destruction de l'habitat (urbanisation, comblement, mise en culture), évolution naturelle vers un stade de végétation ultérieur, invasions biologiques, perturbations dues à la faune sauvage, mauvaise gestion du pâturage

pressures_info:
The pressures and threats that act on the sturgeon species have been compiled based on the results from the long-running research (over 8 years) on the behavior and migration routes of sturgeon species made by the INCDPM-Bucharest (Deak Gy. et al., 2014, 2017, 2018; Danalache T., et al., 2017, 2018; Raischi M., et al., 2017, INCDPM – all the research studies from 2011-2019). 
Regarding the situation of Huso huso, following the unique results obtained by INCDPM Bucharest, it is confirmed that poaching or illegal fishing is currently recognized as one of the most aggressive problems at the level of the Lower Danube river sector that acts on the conservation status. The monitoring processes carried out by INCDPM Bucharest revealed that, for the Huso huso, approximately 56% of the marked and monitored individuals were lost due to poaching (Deak Gy et al., 2014, 2017, 2018; Danalache T ., et al., 2017, 2018; Raischi M., et al., 2017, INCDPM - all research studies from 2011-2019).

pressures_info: Illegal hunting is an increasing problem, and has been estimated to approximately 20% of the population, and increased during this reporting period. It is common where wolves are abundant. There are some new immigrants reproducing in the population that will reduce the population's inbreeding coefficient and genetic depression since the population is inbred and effects on fecundity have been found. In addition to the FRP value, Sweden therefore also applies criterion for genetically effective immigration as a FCS citerion for its wolf population. In about 50% of the reported range (Reindeer husbandry area), wolves usually become legally hunted very soon (derogation HD Art. 16), and no wolves have been allowed to reproduce in this area and hence hunting induces pressure there.
pressures_source:
Scandulv
Swedish Environmental Protection Agency

pressures_info: The species is strongly associated sun exposed dying or recently dead aspen trees. The main overall threat is likely to be degradation or loss of habitat quality, involving structural changes in the tree populations arising from changing land use. Intense forest management focused on coniferous tree production by clear-cutting gradually causes a deterioration suitable habitat and usually prevents the creation of new habitats. Lack of disturbance like forest fires and flooding prevents aspen rich successions. Furthermore, beaver Castor fiber, elk Alces alces and roe deer Capreolus capreolus, grazers of aspen, may locally become a threat to the species. Drainage are also an issue.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info: The species is threatened by ceased grazing, mainly because the old trees suffer from competition and shading from the younger ones. Shading also changes the microclimate inside the tree hollows in a negative way. Ceased grazing often results in conversion to other forest habitats. Remaining hollow trees in the managed forest need much better management consideration. Many localities also lack trees to be the next generation of hollow trees. This will result in a gap in time when no suitable hollow trees are available. This is already a problem in many places and will lead to a deficiency of hollow trees in a near future. Removal of old trees is locally a pressure but diminishing due to better information.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info:
"The code Xo (Threats and pressures from outside the Member State) is used for ‘% of the rough nitrogen deposition from outside the Flanders region’. For other pollutants and for human-induced changes in water regimes, we lack data to link these with key sectoral driver(s). In many cases they are related to several causes. As such they are reported under the categories J and K.
It is very curious that no ""lack of connectivity"" threat and/or pressure is mentioned in the official lists! In this case this is a major pressure and threat for the habitat that only still exists on very limited and scattered places!"

pressures_info: IAS: Robinia pseudoacacia, Ailanthus altissima.

The pressures detected and communicated in previous sexennial reports are becoming less relevant, due to the application of the Natura 2000 sites management plans,  in which this hábitat is mostly located. Therefore, and since the category of less importance (L) has been deleted, it is considered that there are no pressures noteworthy. In relation to the threats, the main ones for this habitat are those that appear in any of the predicted climate change scenarios (see http://climaimpacto.eu/efectos/escenarios-climaticos-en-canarias/), although the uncertainty is still too high to be considered as threats of medium importance.

On the other hand, the existing fire extinguishing system makes fire (codes A11, H04 and M09) to be considered a threat of only minor importance

pressures_info:
Des menaces biotiques et abiotiques naturelles pèsent sur l'ensemble des stations connues. Cependant, des efforts conséquents sont faits par de nombreux gestionnaires pour restaurer cet habitat et, au vu des résultats parfois spectaculaires obtenus, il est important de souligner que les potentialités de restauration demeurent très grandes sur de nombreux sites, en particulier dans le nord de la France. Cependant, la baisse de la qualité des eaux superficielles et la diminution dramatique de la fréquence et de la durée des inondations hivernales dans beaucoup de sites littoraux depuis les années 1990 font peser de lourdes menaces sur cet habitat, dont le reboisement spontané s'accélère dans les sites non gérés (parfois au profit de l'habitat 2170, mais surtout des saulaies du 2180) ou dont les bas-marais se transforment en végétations eutrophiles perdant leur spécificité littorale. 
Notons également que malgré l'existence de nombreux dispositifs réglementaires ayant pour objectif de protéger les milieux côtiers, les écosystèmes dunaires restent menacés par l'extension ponctuelle de l'urbanisation, des aménagements industriels ou portuaires et par l'intensification de certaines activités de loisirs sur le littoral (atteintes directes ou indirectes).

pressures_info:
The illegal hunting is mostly a problem in the Alpine region where conflicts with reindeer husbandry are abundant.
Hunting to protect reindeer husbandry interests is quite common.
There are no pressures and threats identified for Myotis brandtii.

The lists of pressures and threats are based on expert judgements and on published sources. Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), and more knowledge and data. The species mainly occurs in semi-natural grasslands and the recorded pressures and threats are consistent with the assessment made for those habitats.

The main threat is efficient fire-suppression in modern forestry. Also withdrawal of fire damaged trees after forest fires is a threat. An increasing threat is completion from more opportunistic beetles, using the same substrate.

Expert judgment and literature

Literature and expert judgment. Beside the pressures responsible for the earlier declines some new ones have faced during the 21th century: a warmer climate/droughts and increase of short term regulation in rivers used for hydro power. These stressors are affecting M. margaritifera in all regions in Sweden.

The lists of pressures and threats are based on expert judgements, discussions with the reference group and on published sources. Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), as well as more knowledge and data.

pressures_info: Asp populations are threatened mostly by fragmentation of larger rivers and deficiency of suitable spawning sites. Asp occurs in most drinking water reservoirs but its populations is mostly dependent on artificial stocking therrein. The Asp is widely reared and stocked in rivers by angling union as well. Unfortunatelly, all this stock originates from limited numbers of individuals from few river basinsand that can lead to decrease of intraspecies genetic diversity.

pressures_info: Durante el año 2000 se comprobó que el Parvovirus de la Enfermedad Aleutiana (ADV), es una enfermedad que afectaba a un alto porcentaje de la población de visón europeo que, según zonas, va desde un 33% (Navarra y Álava) de los ejemplares analizados, a más de los 65% (La Rioja y Burgos) (Palazón, 2017). Sin embargo, estudios recientes restan importancia a tal enfermedad ya que las poblaciones sanas de visón europeo poseen resistencia natural al patógeno y por tanto éste no supone una amenaza letal para la supervivencia de la especie, si bien otra amenaza potencial (Mañas, 2016).
pressures_source: La expansión del visón americano (Neovison vison) en el área de distribución del visón europeo ha sido la principal presión para la especie. El área de distribución del visón americano en España se duplicó cada 5 años entre 1985 y 2012. En 2012, más de ¼ del país estaba colonizado y la especie americana había invadido 1/3 del área histórica del visón europeo (Põdra & Gómez, 2018). El visón americano aparece en esta zona proveniente de cuencas fluviales francesas y se limita por el momento a la vertiente atlántica. Se viene realizando un trabajo de detección y eliminación del mismo en Navarra, con el resultado de 1 individuo capturado en el periodo 2016-2017 en el río Luzaide. Los trabajos de detección en la vertiente mediterránea no han detectado ningún individuo.

pressures_info: El listado de presiones se ha obtenido a través de una superposición espacial entre los mapas de distribución de la especie y los mapas de las presiones, junto con información sobre la vulnerabilidad de la especie a cada presión mapeada. Para la evaluación de amenazas, se utilizó la opinión de expertos para evaluar la viabilidad de las presiones actuales para seguir impactando en los próximos 12 años.

Other IAS: la introducción de especies exóticas en su área de ocupación como son el pez sol (Lepomis gibbosus), el pez gato (Ameiurus melas), perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), lucio (Esox lucius), lucioperca (Stizostedion lucioperca) y especies de ciprínidos, es la causa principal del declive de esta especie.

Other IAS: Las especies que se mencionan a continuación, son especies introducidas de manera ilegal que están generando graves problemas a la fauna piscícola autóctona, ya que la mayoría no tienen depredadores y además su tasa de reproducción es muy alta. Dichas especies son: carpa (Cyprinus carpio), lucio (Esox lucius), blackbass o perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), salmón del Danubio (Hucho hucho), gambusia (Gambusia holbrooki), perca-sol (Lepomis gibbosus), trucha arcoiris (Oncothynchus mykiss), pez gato negro (Ameiurus melas) y siluro (Silurus glanis).

pressures_source:
Se ha reportado un caso de depredación de lagarto verdinegro por parte de gatos asilvestrados en Galicia (Galán, 2013). Dada la ubicuidad de estos animales domésticos es predecible que la magnitud de este problema de conservación sea más grande de lo esperado (https://www.udc.es/grupos/gibe/uploads/gibe/pedro%20galan/1%20-%20Galan.pdf).
Se ha determinado que las plantaciones de monocultivos de eucaliptos (Eucaliptus spp) y coníferas están causando un daño serio sobre hábitats riparios en el Noroeste de España y Portugal (Marco, 1997; Brito et al., 1995; Godinho & Brito, 2008).

Las principales amenazas y presiones se relacionan con el manejo intensivo de los bosques. Esta especie está ligada a ecosistemas forestales maduros por lo que la destrucción y pérdida de la biomasa forestal por talas, gestión y sobreexplotación forestal, uso de pesticidas en pinares y otras acciones empeoran en gran medida el hábitat disponible para la especie (Paz, 2007). De igual forma, en edificaciones antiguas como iglesias, conventos, castillos y molinos abandonados donde la especie tiene refugios y colonias, los tratamientos químicos de la madera ocasionan habitualmente la desaparición de la especie (https://bibliotecadigital.jcyl.es/es/catalogo_imagenes/grupo.cmd?path=10128177).

pressures_info: Otras amenazas a tener en cuenta son la competencia (M), los cambios térmicos (M), la sequía y disminución de la precipitación (M), modificaciones del flujo hidrológico (M) y otras molestias y perturbaciones humanas (M).

Otras presiones a tener en cuenta son las concentraciones parcelarias (H), contaminación difusa de aguas superficiales causada por actividades agrícolas y forestales (L), deshechos y residuos sólidos (L), gestión de la vegetación acuática para facilitar el drenaje (M), disminución o pérdoda de ñas características específicas de un hábitat (L) y desecación (M).

Other IAS: la introducción de especies exóticas en su área de ocupación como son el pez sol (Lepomis gibbosus), el pez gato (Ameiurus melas), perca americana (Micropterus salmoides), lucio (Esox lucius), lucioperca (Stizostedion lucioperca) y diversas especies de ciprínidos es una de las principales causas del declive de esta especie. Destaca la expansión del alburno y su gran capacidad colonizadora y competidora de los hábitats ocupados por la Boga del Guadiana (Almeida et al., 2014, 2016).
Otra presión a tener en cuenta pero de importancia baja (L) es la modificación del flujo de agua o alteración física de cuerpos de agua para la agricultura.

pressures_info: There are no pressures and threats identified for Myotis daubentonii.

The species is threatened by ceased grazing, mainly because the old trees suffer from competition and shading from the younger ones. Shading also changes the microclimate inside the tree hollows in a negative way. Many places also lack trees to be the next generation of hollow trees. This will result in a gap in time when no suitable hollow trees are available. This is already a problem in many places and will lead to a deficiency of hollow trees in a nearly future. Forestry also isolate occurrences by low consideration, i.e. not leaving large enough areas with suitable substrate and leaving few trees that can be future substrate.

"The code Xo ‘Threats and pressures from outside the Member State' is used for '% of the rough nitrogen deposition from outside the Flanders region’. An important fraction of this foreign deposition consists of NH3, which is almost entirely attributable to agriculture. Nevertheless A27 ‘Agricultural activities generating air pollution’ is strictly referring to deposition originating from within Flanders. If one would combine air pollution generated by agricultural activities from inside and outside the Flanders region and abroad, then the importance of A27 may rank higher.
For other pollutants or human-induced changes in water regimes we lack data to link these with key sectoral driver(s). In many cases these are related to several causes. As such these are reported under the categories J and K."

featurename: Fixed coastal dunes with herbaceous vegetation ("grey dunes")
pressures_info:
The pressures detected and communicated in previous sexennial reports are becoming less relevant, due to the application of the Natura 2000 sites management plans,  in which this hábitat is mostly located. Therefore, and since the category of less importance (L) has been deleted, it is considered that there are no pressures noteworthy. In relation to the threats, the main ones for this habitat are those that appear in any of the predicted climate change scenarios (see http://climaimpacto.eu/efectos/escenarios-climaticos-en-canarias/), although the uncertainty is still too high to be considered as threats of medium importance.

On the other hand, the existing fire extinguishing system makes fire (codes A11, H04 and M09) to be considered a threat of only minor importance

pressures_info:
The pressures and threats presented in Section 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium or low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact. For the current reporting period data on pressures were available for four 2140 sites surveyed by Martin (2018c), source referenced above in Section 7.2, with no pressures recorded impacting on the very small 2140 site at Kincaslough that accounts for <1% of the national 2140 area.
Only three pressures were recorded on more than one occasion and all three are listed as medium-importance pressures in Section 7.1.
The one medium-importance pressure recorded during the current reporting period which was not recorded in the previous recording period (NPWS, 2013) was F07 Sports, tourism and leisure activities. There were three medium/high-importance pressures recorded during the previous reporting period (NPWS, 2013) that were not considered to be of medium/high-importance during the current reporting period. One of these, A02 Conversion from one type of agricultural land use to another (excluding drainage and burning), was not recorded during the current reporting period. Another, I02 Other invasive alien species (other than species of Union concern), was recorded but as a neutral impact. The third one, L02 Abiotic natural processes (e.g. erosion, silting up, drying out, submersion, salinization), was recorded as A06 during the current reporting period.
Pressures and threats noted less frequently and/or that impacted only small areas were deemed to be of low importance. The low-importance pressure recorded during the current reporting period was H08 Other human intrusions and disturbance not mentioned above; this code was used to record accidental burning of the 2140 dune heath.
Climate change has not been added as an impact but it is likely to affect the range of 2140 over the coming decades. The short-term predictability of the effect of climate change and in particular the resultant mean sea level rise and extreme weather events is unknown but the gradual long-term trend will see an increase in mean sea level and extreme weather (EPA, 2017) which will affect the 2140 habitat.
The three pressures presented in Section 7.1 are also considered to be the three main threats in the future. In the previous reporting period F08 Modification of coastline, estuary and coastal conditions for development, use and protection of residential, commercial, industrial and recreational infrastructure and areas (including sea defence or coast protection works and infrastructures) was considered to be a threat, but during the current reporting period it was not considered to be a threat as it was not applicable for the areas within the sites where the 2140 habitat was recorded.

pressures_info:
The habitat of E. aurinia has gradually declined during the 20th century. On the islands of Öland and Gotland the habitat is still sufficient concerning area and quality of occupied habitat.  The loss of habitats has been largest on the mainland and the population is today mostly restricted power line clearings. The habitats here occur as patches in areas with mineral rich soil and are often close to small streams, wetlands or surfacing ground water where the bedrock comes close to the ground surface.
The habitat has deteriorated due to changes in management of pastures but also in changes in forestry. Modification of hydrological conditions in agriculture and forestry have affected wet grassland and fens. 
Future threats are similar to the present pressures.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info: The species is threatened by ceased grazing, mainly because the old trees suffer from competition and shading from the younger ones. Many places also lack trees to be the next generation of suitable substrate. This will result in a gap in time when no suitable substrate is available. Forestry also isolate occurrences by low consideration, i.e. leaving large enough areas with suitable substrate and leaving trees that can be future substrate.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info: Myotis nattereri is a forest species and factors connected to forestry are the main threats

The pressure is intensive forestry management which have to low concentrations of dead wood. This species is therefore threatened by intensive forestry management, in particular forestry clearance and the removal of dead and dying trees. Lack of natural disturbance like forest fires is probably a pressure. Also removal of dead trees after disturbance like storms.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info:
Lycaena helle was quite common over most of northern and central Scandinavia as long as agricultural activities were relatively extensive. However, the population has declined strongly during the second half of the 20th century. Previously suitable habitats have been abandoned and overgrown, or transformed into intensively used and fertilized farmland. The changes in hay cutting and harvest is considered especially determinable. This has led to a severe loss of habitats in farmed areas, and subsequent strong fragmentation of the remaining populations. Today the species survive better on limestone influenced and flower-rich wetlands with sparse vegetation than on farmland. The species also occurs in other disturbed "human" habitats like roadsides, power lines, military training fields, and airports.
Future threats are similar to the present pressures, with one exception. Future threats also include climate change as the species has a northern distribution and its southern limit might be pushed northward.
pressures_source: See 4.2.

pressures_info: We do not know how chytridomycosis will affect this species, but it is quite possible it will be rather extensive.

The effects of sand extraction are rather unknown, but may possibly pose a problem. 
The main issuewith wind power may be the noise when building them, also prime habitat may be permanently altered.
Disturbance through noice is the main issue with ferry/shipping lanes.
Noise is always a problem for sonar dependent species, but they are rather vigillant, which means they may be disturbed by more intense leisure activities.
Studies from the UK show that porpoise are very sensitive to dioxines, with reduced fertility as a result. Dioxines are present in the MATL, and the population seems to be reproducing, however the sample sizes are too small to say how much they suffer from this.
Competition for food is the problem with fishing.
Bycatch is rated as medium (compared to the poulation in the baltic). This is due to the larger population size may be less affected by stochastic variation in bycatch. 
Other pollutants may also pose problems for porpoise, however the extent is relatively unknown.

pressures_info:
The pressures detected and communicated in previous sexennial reports are becoming less relevant, due to the application of the Natura 2000 sites management plans,  in which this hábitat is mostly located. Therefore, and since the category of less importance (L) has been deleted, it is considered that there are no pressures noteworthy. In relation to the threats, the main ones for this habitat are those that appear in any of the predicted climate change scenarios (see http://climaimpacto.eu/efectos/escenarios-climaticos-en-canarias/), although the uncertainty is still too high to be considered as threats of medium importance.

On the other hand, the existing fire extinguishing system makes fire (codes A11, H04 and M09) to be considered a threat of only minor importance

pressures_info:
Les menaces les plus fortes sont celles qui induisent des réductions de l'espace de mobilité des cours d'eau, qui permettent la stabilisation de leurs berges ou du lit ou encore qui modifient le régime hydrologique (réduction des étiages estivaux notamment). L'incision des lits fluviaux consécutivement
aux extractions massives de matériaux alluvionnaires (dans le passé) ou de dragage (encore en cours sur certains fleuves) sont aussi pénalisantes pour l'habitat, notamment aux abords des estuaires. Les menaces sont encore plus fortes pour les végétations du Chenopodion rubri, notamment pour ses
associations les moins nitrophiles. La qualité de l'eau (quand elle n'est pas optimale) n'engendre pas de destruction notable de l'habitat au niveau générique, mais ne permet plus l'expression des associations les moins eutrophiles, ce qui est néanmoins fortement dommageable pour la biodiversité et les espèces les plus rares et menacées. L'habitat est aussi susceptible d'être menacé par certaines espèces exotiques envahissantes comme Xanthium gr. orientale, Ludwigia peploides, Paspalum distichum et surtout Ludwigia grandiflora avec sa forme "terrestre" apparue ou reconnue depuis peu (après 2012)... Par ailleurs, quand l'habitat est perturbé par diverses actions anthropiques, il y a des risques de dérive vers d'autres végétations moins intéressantes.

pressures_info: L'évaluation des principales pressions et menaces est essentiellement basé sur du dire d'experts au regard des principales activités humaines qui se développent sur l'habitat générique "Replat boueux ou sableux exondés à marée basse": installations importantes d'activités conchylicoles (ex. baie du Mont-Saint-Michel), pratique importante et en essor de la pêche à pied récréative, développement des activités récréatives qui se traduisent entre autres par une "nécessité" d'entretien des plages et un accroissement de la pollution par les macrodéchets. Situé à l'interface entre milieu continental et milieu marin, il subit de plein fouet les aménagements littoraux en relation avec une urbanisation croissante des zones littorales, et est en conséquence exposé à différentes pollutions. A l'avenir, les menaces principales qui pèsent sur cet habitat sont à mettre en relation avec le changement climatique et en particulier l'élévation du niveau moyen de la mer. S'ajoutent à cela l'érosion du littoral et le développement d'espèces non indigènes telle que la palourde japonaise.

pressures_info: It should be noted that such a geographically and ecologically widespread animal is subject to a wide range of pressures and threats.

There were often more than ten pressures or threats (of high or medium importance) identified, and an aggregation method was used to identify the top ten of each. As a result the top ten lists for the feature may not correspond with each other. For example, a pressure may be in the reported top ten list, but may not appear in the top ten list of threats. This does not necessarily mean that the threat was not listed but instead it is in the extended list of threats that did not make the top ten but are detailed in the additional information section.
The following pressures were also identified, however, a maximum of 10 could be listed: D05- Development and operation of energy production plants (including bioenergy plants, fossil and nuclear energy plants), E07- Land, water and air transport activities generating marine pollution, F07- Sports, tourism and leisure activities, F20- Residential or recreational activities and structures generating marine pollution (excl. marine macro- and micro-particular pollution)
The following threats were also identified, however, a maximum of 10 could be listed: D05- Development and operation of energy production plants (including bioenergy plants, fossil and nuclear energy plants), E07- Land, water and air transport activities generating marine pollution, F07- Sports, tourism and leisure activities, F20- Residential or recreational activities and structures generating marine pollution (excl. marine macro- and micro-particular pollution), F21- Industrial or commercial activities and structures generating marine pollution (excluding marine macro- and micro-particular pollution), A28- Agricultural activities generating marine pollution, C01- Extraction of minerals (e.g. rock, metal ores, gravel, sand, shell), C07- Dumping/depositing of dredged materials from marine extraction, D01- Wind, wave and tidal power, including infrastructure, E02- Shipping lanes and ferry lanes transport operations, J02- Mixed source marine water pollution (marine and coastal)
For methods see JNCC website for 2019 UK Approach Document and country-level reporting information.

pressures_info:
"The code Xo ‘Threats and pressures from outside the Member State’ is used for % of the rough nitrogen deposition from outside the Flanders region’. An important fraction of this foreign deposition consists of NH3, which is almost entirely attributable to agriculture. Nevertheless A27 ‘Agricultural activities generating air pollution’ is strictly referring to deposition originating from within Flanders. If one would combine air pollution generated by agricultural activities from inside and outside the Flanders regionand abroad, then the importance of A27 may rank higher.
For other pollutants or human-induced changes in water regimes we lack data to link these with key sectoral driver(s). In many cases these are related to several causes. As such these are reported under the categories J and K."

pressures_info:
The pressures detected and communicated in previous sexennial reports are becoming less relevant, due to the application of the Natura 2000 sites management plans,  in which this hábitat is mostly located. Therefore, and since the category of less importance (L) has been deleted, it is considered that there are no pressures noteworthy. In relation to the threats, the main ones for this habitat are those that appear in any of the predicted climate change scenarios (see http://climaimpacto.eu/efectos/escenarios-climaticos-en-canarias/), although the uncertainty is still too high to be considered as threats of medium importance.

On the other hand, the existing fire extinguishing system makes fire (codes A11, H04 and M09) to be considered a threat of only minor importance

Cet habitat a souffert de nombreuses atteintes par le passé (déprise pastorale, enrésinement, modification des dynamiques fluviales, extraction de granulats, urbanisation, infrastructure touristique, disparition des lapins...). Dans l'ensemble, les atteintes récentes et menaces les plus importantes sont liées à la fragmentation de l'habitat et à la dynamique de stabilisation / fermeture de ces pelouses pionnières. featurename: Salicornia and other annuals colonizing mud and sand
pressures_info:
Pressures and threats in section 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium or low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact, using the data gathered during the 2017-18 SMP and presented in Brophy et al. (in prep.), as well as reviewing other sources of information.
All pressures presented in NPWS (2013) were considered for the current reporting period. Of the 10 high or medium-ranked pressures in NPWS (2013), only two of the high-importance pressures were retained for the current monitoring period and both were reduced to medium importance. Invasive non-native species (previously I01, cross-walked to I02 in the current impact recording scheme) relates to Spartina anglica. As noted in NPWS (2013), Spartina anglica poses a threat to stands of Salicornia spp. through direct competition for pioneer habitat and it was recorded as a pressure at 25 sites in the current survey. However, there is no evidence to suggest that this species is expanding at the national level and therefore the pressure is considered to be of Medium importance.  Intensive cattle grazing (A04.01.01) was previously ranked as being of high importance; however A09 Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock in the new scheme was ranked as medium importance. This pressure was recorded at three sites, with high or medium intensity, and affected 50-100% of the habitat. Of the other pressures recorded in the previous reporting period, species composition change (succession) (K02.01) was occasionally recorded as L02 Natural succession resulting in species composition change (other than by direct changes of agricultural or forestry practices) in the 2017-18 survey where 1310 had become 1330, but this was not assessed as it is considered a natural process. Similarly, Erosion (previously K01.01, now under L01) was considered a natural process and so was not assessed. The remaining pressures recorded in the previous reporting period were not noted during the 2017-18 survey. For some, this may be that they were not occurring at the sites surveyed, while others are difficult to define and assess, such as silting up (K01.02) or diffuse household pollution (H01.08).
Climate change has not been added as an impact of medium or high importance, but it is likely to affect the range of 1310 over the coming decades. The long-term trend will see an increase in mean sea level and extreme weather events (Desmond et al., 2017), which will impact the formation and stabilisation of the 1310 habitat.
The two pressures presented in section 7.1 are also considered to be the two main threats into the future.

pressures_info:
Les principales menaces pour les boisements alluviaux en place sont la modification des conditions hydrauliques, notamment par l'endiguement des fleuves (chenalisation), et le morcellement. 
Selon le niveau topographique occupé et l'humidité du substrat, l'habitat sera plus ou moins affecté par la baisse du niveau de la nappe alluviale. L'artificialisation des peuplements par replantation d'autres essences (peupliers, résineux, essences allochtones) et l'élimination des arbres sénescents ou le traitement en futaie régulière sont également néfastes pour ces forêts alluviales. 
Dans certains cas, il est nécessaire de lutter contre la prolifération d'espèces invasives. 
Pour les forêts relictuelles fragmentées (forêts galeries discontinues ou boisements isolés) il est nécessaire de restaurer des continuums forestiers pour permettre les flux génétiques et le maintien de la biodiversité.

pressures_info:
The pressures and threats presented in Section 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium or low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact. Data gathered from the sources listed in Section 7.2 are summarised in Martin (2018). For the current reporting period, data on pressures were available for twenty-eight 1220 sites, 27 surveyed by Martin et al. (2017) and one by Devaney et al. (2014).
All pressures recorded during the current reporting period were converted from the old recording system to the new, using the crosswalk referenced in Martin (2018). Only six pressures were recorded on more than two occasions. F07 was the most commonly recorded pressure noted 25 times (seven as a neutral impact) under the impacts ‘storage of boats and fishing equipment’, ‘horse riding, walking’, ‘trampling’, ‘off-road driving’ and ‘camping’. The other five most frequently recorded pressures were F09 recorded 22 times, F08 recorded 19 times (nine as a neutral impact), I02 recorded 11 times, C01 recorded six times and E01 recorded four times. Although F08 was not the most frequently recorded pressure it did impact large areas of the 1220 habitat and was often a high-intensity pressure and for these reasons ‘sea defences and coastal protection works’ were considered to be the one high-importance pressure for the 1220 habitat. The other five frequently recorded pressures were judged to be of medium importance.
There were two medium-importance pressures recorded during the current reporting period which were not recorded in the previous recording period (NPWS, 2013); these were E01 Roads, paths, railroads and related infrastructure (e.g. bridges, viaducts, tunnels) in the form of tracks and paved roads, and I02 Other invasive alien species (other than species of Union concern). All medium/high-importance pressures recorded during the previous reporting period (NPWS, 2013) were recorded during the current reporting period.
Pressures and threats noted less frequently and/or that impacted only small areas were deemed to be of low importance. Low-importance impacts recorded during the current reporting period included A36 Agriculture activities not referred to above in the form of fences, C06 Dumping/depositing of inert materials from terrestrial extraction, F01 Conversion from other land uses to housing, settlement or recreational areas (excluding drainage and modification of coastline, estuary and coastal conditions) in the form of a carpark, and I04 Problematic native species. Impacts of low importance recorded during the previous reporting period (NPWS, 2013) which were not recorded during the current reporting period included D07 Oil and gas pipelines (recorded under the impact ‘pipelines’) and F10 Deposition and treatment of waste/garbage from commercial and industrial facilities (recorded under the impact ‘disposal of inert material). It should be noted that it was not possible to crosswalk the NPWS (2013) recorded impact H07 Other forms of pollution to the new 2017 pressure codes as there was no matching code.
Climate change has not been added as an impact but it is likely to affect the range of 1220 over the coming decades. The short-term predictability of the effect of climate change and in particular the resultant mean sea level rise and extreme weather events is unknown but the gradual long-term trend will see an increase in mean sea level and extreme weather (EPA, 2017) which will affect the 1220 habitat. JNCC (2004) states that the shingle structures that support the 1220 habitat develop from the high tide limit to more permanent structures created by storm waves which form high up the beach, thus a rise in mean sea levels will affect the habitat, particularly where it forms at the high tide limit. Furthermore, 1220 requires at least three years’ stability to form (Scott, 1963); thus, frequent extreme weather events could affect the formation of this habitat.
The six pressures presented in Section 7.1 are also considered to be the six main threats in the future.

featurename: Sandbanks which are slightly covered by sea water all the time
pressures_info:
No visible pressures were recorded during field surveys between 2016 and 2018.
Threats were selected based on best expert judgement.
Changes to the habitat as a result of natural causes, e.g. natural forces leading to a change in the area or general topographical feature of the habitat, are considered a neutral impact.
Climate change is considered a threat to this habitat although sufficient information is not available to discern the nature and severity of this threat. 
The development of windfarms on shallow sandbanks has the potential to lead to an indirect impact on the habitat. 
Impacts from benthic dredging (fisheries) are also considered to be a potential threat to this habitat. The development of Natura Fisheries Plans as required under “Fisheries Act” should mitigate the risk to this habitat.

featurename: Boreal Baltic islets and small islands
pressures_info: Eutrophication  causes  less light penetration and risk of overgrowth of filamentous algae, which effects the vegetation. Disturbance, noise from jets etc. Leakage of toxic hull antifauling (TBT) despite long term ban.  Point sources from seawage plants  and also diffuse leakage of sewer from individual households and summer cottages due to substandard sewage treatment is problematic in certain  coastal areas causing nutrient  (nitrogen) load.  The harvesting of fish resulting in a shortage of large carnivorious fish have clear top down effects strenghtening the effects of eutrophication. Internal load of nutrients (phosphorus) limits the  effects of decreased terrestrial load of nutrients. Unknown cause for  general fish health detoriation along the coastal Baltic Sea. Also unknown pressure affecting  mussel health (body weight affrecting ), biogenic reefs  and  musseleating species. Exact figures on how much plastic that is deposited at different shores is hard to get, and there are in some areas a (but uneven) NGO work on clening the shores from plastics and other rubbish. Ongoing development in most parts of archipelago, but most frequent close to the big cities in Sweden.

The lists of pressures and threats are based on expert judgements and on published sources. The lists were discussed and revised together with a reference group of professionals working with research, administration, and active conservation management in semi-natural grasslands. Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), and more knowledge and data. 
However, the problem with alien invasive species (especially Rosa rugosa) has increased over the last few years and is likely to escalate in the future (Strand et al. 2018).

The lists of pressures and threats are based on expert judgements and on published sources. Changes from previous reports are mainly due to a different method (as given by the guidelines), and more knowledge and data. 
However, the problem with alien invasive species (especially Rosa rugosa) has increased over the last few years and is likely to escalate in the future (Strand et al. 2018). IO2 is therefore assessed as a medium impact pressure and threat. Rosa rugosa is assumed to be less of a problem in 2190 than in drier dune habitats.

pressures_info:
There were often more than ten pressures, threats (of high or medium importance), or conservation measures identified, and an aggregation method was used to identify the top ten of each. As a result the top ten lists for the habitat may not correspond with each other. For example, a pressure may be in the reported top ten list, but the corresponding conservation measure might not appear in the top ten list of conservation measures. This does not mean that the measure is not in place, but instead it is in the extended list of measures that did not make the top ten but are detailed in the additional information section. 
The following threats were identified as medium importance, however, a maximum of ten could be reported: A28-Agricultural activities generating marine pollution, N05-Change of habitat location, size, and / or quality due to climate change, E01-Roads, paths, railroads and related infrastructure (e.g. bridges, viaducts, tunnels).
For details on approaches used in this section see JNCC website for 2019 UK Approach Document and relevant country-level reporting information.

featurename: Shifting dunes along the shoreline with Ammophila arenaria ("white dunes")
pressures_info:
The pressures detected and communicated in previous sexennial reports are becoming less relevant, due to the application of the Natura 2000 sites management plans,  in which this hábitat is mostly located. Therefore, and since the category of less importance (L) has been deleted, it is considered that there are no pressures noteworthy. In relation to the threats, the main ones for this habitat are those that appear in any of the predicted climate change scenarios (see http://climaimpacto.eu/efectos/escenarios-climaticos-en-canarias/), although the uncertainty is still too high to be considered as threats of medium importance.

On the other hand, the existing fire extinguishing system makes fire (codes A11, H04 and M09) to be considered a threat of only minor importance

pressures_info:
Actually, no pressures means that only pressures with low ranking act to the priority habitat type 6260.
The major threats to the habitat type are water level fluctuations caused by anthropogenic influences (e.g. excavations, embankments), invasion of non-native species and the absence of grazing. We assume that any further increase in the water level of the lakes will negatively affect the *6260 habitat type in the study area.
The existence of short, shrub-like trees of Salix alba as well as of trees like Robinia pseudoacacia and Morus spp., planted at the localities of the habitat type, may be a threat because of the possible invasion of these species in the habitat. The invasion of Robinia pseudoacacia has been recorded as a serious threat for *6260 in Slovakia (Šefferova Stanova et al. 2008). This species, due to its intensive vegetative spread and its high germination rates after fire, becomes a superior competitor in secondary succession processes.
The study area is only occasionally grazed by cattle. Abandonment of grazing may lead to closed grasslands and increased moss cover (Molnar 2003). 
In Lake Megali Prespa the water level cannot be artificially controlled, but the areas occupied by the habitat type do not appear threatened by a potential water level rise as they lie 3–5 m above the present water level.
pressures_source: Fotiadis G., M. Vrahnakis, Y. Kazoglou & I. Tsiripidis. 2014. Dry grassland types in the Prespa National Park (NW Greece), including the southernmost occurrence of the priority habitat type “Pannonic sand steppes” (code 6260). HACQUETIA 13/1: p. 171-189 (DOI: 10.2478/hacq-2014-0013).

pressures_info:
Pressures and threats in section 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium or low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact, using the data gathered during the 2017-18 SMP and presented in Brophy et al. (in prep.), as well as reviewing other sources of information. 
All pressures presented in NPWS (2013) were considered for the current reporting period. Of the two high or medium-ranked pressures in NPWS (2013), the high-ranking pressure of intensive grazing was retained for the current monitoring period, while the medium-ranked pressure of D01 Paths, tracks, cycling tracks was assessed as low importance and so was not included in section 7.1. Intensive cattle grazing (A04.01.01) was previously ranked in NPWS (2013) as being of high importance, and this cross-walked to A09 Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock in the new impact recording scheme and so continues to be ranked as high importance. This was the most frequently recorded pressure, being noted at 40 sites, usually at high or medium intensity, and affected on average >25% of the habitat.
D01.01 cross-walked to the F07 Recreational activities code, and was ranked as low importance in the current survey. This pressure was recorded at three sites, usually with a high or medium intensity, but affected on average <1% of the habitat. D01.01 recorded in NPWS (2013) may also comprise tracks created by the operation of agricultural vehicles, which were included in the current assessment as A36 and was ranked as medium importance (recorded at six sites and almost always as high intensity). From this it can be assumed that the apparent change in impacts from the previous recording period to the current is likely be the result of a change in methodology rather than a change in the pressure acting on the habitat. 
A33 (recorded at three sites at high intensity in the current survey) relates to infilling, reclamation, construction and repair of embankments, etc., for agriculture. A10 Extensive grazing or undergrazing by livestock was assessed as being of medium importance despite only occurring at one site (Castlebridge SMP0038). This was due to the fact that it accounted for 73% (11.3ha) of the area loss of 1410 recorded. This area of 1410 has been transformed to Phragmites australis swamp due to a reduction in, or cessation of, grazing at the site.
Climate change has not been added as an impact of medium or high importance, but it is likely to affect the range of 1410 over the coming decades. The long-term trend will see an increase in mean sea level and extreme weather events (Desmond et al., 2017), which will impact the formation of the 1410 habitat.
The four pressures presented in section 7.1 are also considered to be the four main threats into the future.

pressures_info:
The pressures and threats presented in 7.1 were selected and ranked based on intensity (high, medium or low), frequency of occurrence, and area of impact. Data gathered from the sources listed in Section 7.2 are summarised in Martin (2018). For the current reporting period, data on pressures were available for eight sites, six sites surveyed by Martin et al. (2017), one site by Devaney et al. (2014) and one site by Wilson and Duff (2014). As this represents only a small proportion of the data collected from 36 sites by Delaney et al. (2013) to inform NPWS (2013), the pressure and threats information presented in NPWS (2013) were also utilised to inform the current reporting period.
All pressures presented in NPWS (2013) were considered for the current reporting period. For eight of the pressures presented in NPWS (2013) there was additional evidence provided for their importance by the data collected during the current reporting period. Using the crosswalk referenced in Martin (2018) to transfer pressures to the current recording system, these eight pressures crosswalked to F07 (recorded five times in the current reporting period), F08 (recorded three times, but twice as a neutral pressure), F09, I02, and L01 (each recorded twice during the current reporting period), and F06 and L02 (each recorded once during the current reporting period). Based on the evidence collected over the last two reporting periods and expert opinion, F07, F08 and L01 were retained as high-importance pressures, and F06 and L02 were retained as medium-importance pressures. Although F09, recorded under ‘litter’, and I02 were both recorded twice during the current reporting period, based on the evidence collected during the current reporting period and expert opinion both are considered to be currently low-importance pressures for the 2110 habitat. Both F09 and I02 were reported as medium-importance pressures during NPWS (2013). During the current reporting period no evidence was provided for the medium-importance pressures C01, E03 and F01; however, based on expert opinion all three are still considered to be medium-importance pressures for the 2110 habitat. The pressures ‘Agricultural activities not referred to above’ and ‘Paths, tracks, cycling tracks’ were both listed as medium-importance pressures in NPWS (2013) but based on expert opinion and the fact that both activities have very little impact at the front of dune systems they were not included in the medium-importance pressures listed above in Section 7.1. 
The only low-importance impact recorded in NPWS (2013) was A09 Intensive grazing or overgrazing by livestock and this was retained as a low-importance pressure for the current reporting period.
Climate change was ranked as a high pressure and threat for 2110 in NPWS (2013); however, it has not been added as an impact in this reporting period but it is likely to affect the range of 2110 over the coming decades. The short-term predictability of the effect of climate change and in particular the resultant mean sea level rise and extreme weather events are unknown but the gradual long-term trend will see an increase in the mean sea level and extreme weather events (EPA, 2017) which will affect the 2110 habitat. Rodwell (2000) describes the establishment of this pioneer habitat through the colonisation, by seed and rhizome fragments, of Elytrigia juncea onto small areas of sand from more established stands of the species. As mean sea levels rise and extreme weather events increase, this habitat will be threatened by these combined events removing established areas of the 2110 habitat and reducing niches where species such as Elytrigia juncea can re-establish the habitat.
The eight pressures presented in Section 7.1 are also considered to be the eight main threats in the future.
